name: HMS Una (N 87)
events: The history of HMS Una as compiled on this page is extracted from this submarine's patrol reports and logbooks. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was first created in February 2013.
This page was last updated in December 2025.
8 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) proceeded from her builders yard at Chatham to Sheerness. (1)
9 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted D/G, steering and diving trials of Sheerness. (1)

10 Oct 1941 Around 1415 A/10, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Sheerness for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS Cottesmore (Lt.Cdr. J.C.A. Ingram, RN) towards Dungeness where she was to turn back.

From Dungeness HMS Una proceeded unescorted to Lizard Head where she was joined by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) around 0705 A/13 which was to escort her northwards through the Irish Sea to Holy Loch. (1)
15 Oct 1941 Around 0800 A/15, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) arrived at Holy Loch for a period of trials and training.
She had anchored in Rothesay Bay from 0110 A/15 to 0630 A/15.
Later that morning and in the early afternoon trials were carried out. (1)
17 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted independent exercises in Lower Loch Long. (1)
18 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
19 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
21 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) shifted from Holy Loch to Arrochar. (1)
22 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
23 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
24 Oct 1941 Having completed her torpedo discharge trials, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN), proceeded from Arrochar to Holy Loch. (1)
26 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted noise trials in Lower Loch Long. (1)
27 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted D/G trails off Helensburg. (1)
29 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted practice attacks in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) as target. These included night exercises. (1)
30 Oct 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted practice attacks in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) as target. (1)
1 Nov 1941 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) conducted diving trials in Loch Long followed by compass adjustment trials at Holy Loch. (1)
4 Nov 1941 Around 1800 A/4, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay.
She was escorted until of Wolf Rock by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) which parted company at 1930 A/6. (2)
4 Nov 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 1st war patrol see the map below.

7 Nov 1941 In the evening, a signal from the Flag Officer (S) (timed 1339 A/7) was received indicating the presence of a U-boat and HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) steered to intercept in position 47°10'N, 06°00'W. Nothing was seen though.
[We were unable to find the precise text of this signal.] (2)

10 Nov 1941 At 0001 A/10, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) proceeded to intercept in position 45°45'N 04°23'W a U-boat reported by Flag Officer (S) in his signal timed 1711 A/9 and also in a signal from the Admiralty timed 2209 A/9. The area was reached at 0610 A/10 hours but nothing was seen.
The signal timed 2209 A/9 from the Admiralty started the following; 'D/F bearings at 2018 G.M.T. indicate Italian U/Boat in area 46°N to 47°N., 6°W to 8°W., inward bound.'
This must have referred to the Italian submarine Barbarigo.
[We were unable to find the text of the signal timed 1711 A/9.] (3)

10 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1453 A/10, the Capt. S.3 ordered HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) to leave patrol after dark 12th proceed diving by day through positions 46°01'N, 05°30'W and 47°59'N, 06°00'W to R/V with HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) in position 270° - Wolf Rock - 3 miles at 0800 A/14. (3)

11 Nov 1941 At 0512 B/11 [Rome time], in position 45°34'N, 02°24'W, the Italian submarine Barbarigo sighted an enemy submarine. Barbarigo was not in a good attacking position and turned away. The submarine sighted by the Italians was HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) patrolling off the Gironde to intercept a Bordeaux bound Italian submarine. The British did not notice the Italian submarine.

12 Nov 1941 At 1931 A/12, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) received the Flag Officer (S)'s signal timed 1836 A/12 ordering her to patrol in the vicinity of position 44°16'N, 05°30'W (north of Gijon, Spain).
This signal also cancelled the orders given in Capt. S.3's signal timed 1453 A/10.
In a follow up signal timed 1857 A/12, HMS Una was ordered to keep a good look out for eastbound merchant ships. U-Boat may also be met in this area. (3)

14 Nov 1941 At 1940 A/14, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) left patrol in accordance with Capt. S.3's signal timed 2343 A/13 which ordered her to leave patrol after dark 14th diving by day through position 48°N, 07°W to R/V with HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN) in position 250° - Trevose Head - 10 miles at 0800 A/18. (3)

18 Nov 1941 At 0740 A/18, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) made rendezvous off Trevose Head with HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN) which was to escort her northwards through the Irish Sea to Holy Loch. (2)
20 Nov 1941 At 0930 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Holy Loch.
She had spent the night of 19/20 November in Rothesay Bay where she had anchored at 2020 A/19 leaving for Holy Loch at 0750 A/20. (2)

5 Dec 1941 Around 1600 A/5, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay and to proceed to Gibraltar upon completion of the patrol to join the 10th Submarine Flotilla at Malta.

She was escorted south through the Irish Sea by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). They parted company around 2230 A/7 west-north-west of the Scilly Islands in approximate position 50°00'N, 06°30'W.

HMS Una was to proceed submerged by day through position 49°20'N, 10°30'W, 46°00'N, 10°30'W and 45°00'N, 09°00'W for patrol between between latitudes 44°N and 45°N and between longitudes 09°W and 07°W.

HMS Una was to leave the patrol area after dark on 14 December and proceed diving by day through position 44°00'N, 09°00'W to position 43°00'N, 10°30'W. E.T.A. 0800 A/15 and then to proceed to Gibraltar as ordered by F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic). (4)
5 Dec 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 2nd war patrol see the map below.
14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1106 A/14, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN).
(1) Report position, course and speed of advance on crossing 43°00'N.

(2) HMS P 35 onward route through the following positions; Position L = 43°00'N, 12°40'W Position M = 36°30'N, 10°00'W Thence to R/V with escort in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles Dive by day on the 17th and 18th

(3) HMS Una onward route through the following positions; Position N = 43°00'N, 10°50'W Position O = 40°00'N, 11°00'W Position P = 37°30'N, 11°00'W Position Q = 36°30'N, 10°00'W Thence to R/V with escort in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles Dive by day on the 17th (5)

14 Dec 1941 At 1900 A/14, while north-west of Ferrol, Spain, in position 44°01'N, 09°00'W, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) left patrol to proceed to Gibraltar in accordance with her patrol orders. (4)

14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1618 A/14, the Admiral Submarines sent the following to HMS Regent (Lt. W.N.R. Knox, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Keep a good look out for any heavy enemy warships which may be taking advantage of weather to break out from Brest to the south-westward.

In a follow up signal timed 1949 A/14, the Admiral Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Regent; My 1618 A/14. Proceed to patrol in the vicinity of position 46°20'N, 09°00'W for 24 hours continuing your passage to Portsmouth after dark tomorrow. (5)
15 Dec 1941 At 1111 A/15, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) sighted a torpedo track in position 42°55'N, 10°31'W but this attack was apparently bogus.

Following a B-Dienst (German intelligence) intercept of 10 December, the submarine HMS Regent (Lt. W.N.R. Knox, DSC, RN) was to pass through German naval grid BF 7795 (the center of this grid was in 43°03'N, 10°30'W, which was remarkably close to the 'attack'), BDU had ordered U-105 to try to intercept but she reported that she was not able to carry out this order owing to fuel shortage. U-332 was ordered to intercept instead of U-105. U-332 did not sight any enemy submarine and did not make an attack. On 13 December 1941, U-332 was ordered to continue her passage so she was no longer in the area as well.
The reported torpedo may have been a dolphin or porpoise. (4)

15 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2306 A/15, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) and referring to his signal timed 1106 A/14 ordered HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) to adjust speed to pass through position Q at 0300 A/18. She was also to proceed dived by day on 18th instead of on the 17th. (5)

16 Dec 1941 At 1707 A/16, in position 39°43'N, 11°03'W, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) observed smoke bearing 168°. The source of the smoke was closed and was identified as the Spanish merchant vessel Monte Gorbea (3720 GRT, built 1923). (4)

17 Dec 1941 At 1110 A/17, in position 37°59'N, 10°58'W, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) observed smoke bearing 030°. The source of the smoke was closed and was identified as the Portugese merchant vessel Saudades (3566 GRT, built 1914). (4)

18 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1906 A/18, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic), referring to his signal timed 1406 A/16, informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) that her escort towards Gibraltar would be HMS Spiraea (T/Lt. R.W. Tretheway, RNR). HMS Una was to report if unable to comply within one hour from the rendezvous time. She was also informed that several enemy submarines were operating to the west of Gibraltar.
In a signal timed 2206 A/18, F.O.C.N.A. informed HMS Una that HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN) would pass through position 34°08'N, 09°44'W at 2000 A/18 and thence Gibraltar 15 knots. (5)

18 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2336 A/18, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) that HMS Blankney (Lt.Cdr. P.F. Powlett, DSC, RN) and HMS Exmoor (Lt.Cdr. L.StG. Rich, RN) would pass through position 36°14'N, 14°40'W at 1430 A/18 to Gibraltar at 18 knots. They were also informed that the destroyer were aware of the submarines presence. (5)
19 Dec 1941 Around 1700 A/19, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Gibraltar.
She was escorted in by HMS Heliotrope (Lt.Cdr. J. Jackson, RNR) which had joined around 1150 A/19, in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles. (4)
26 Dec 1941 Around 1215 A/26, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Gibraltar for a direct passage to Malta.
On departure exercises were carried out to the east of Gibraltar.
For the daily positions of HMS Una during this passage see the map below.

29 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1131 B/29 and later amended in a signal timed 2045 B/29, the Vice-Admiral Malta ordered HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) to proceed through the following positions; B = 37°54'N, 11°53'E C = 37°20'N, 12°50'E D = 37°10'N, 13°12'E E = 36°10'N, 14°00'E Una was also to adjust speed to proceed submerged between positions B and C during daylight of 2 January and thence by the south-east searched channels to arrive Marsamxett harbour, Malta before 1800 B/3 making an E.T.A. signal when east of 14°E.
In the same signal HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was ordered to follow the same route as HMS Una but 24 hours later. (5)

1 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1450 B/1, Capt. S.10 referring the the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1131 B/29 informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) of the following; Owing to enemy fighter activity during daylight HMS Una was now to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0800 B/4.

She was also informed that submarines encountered on opposite courses during nights of 2nd/3rd and 3rd/4th were not to be attacked as HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) might be encountered. (7)

2 Jan 1942 At 1143 B/2, south of Marsala, Sicily, in approximate position 37°34'N, 12°24'E, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) felt two explosions which were believed to be aircraft bombs. They were not close though. (6)

2 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1655 B/2, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; The sailing of HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) has been postponed by 24 hours. (7)

3 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1049 B/3, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) leave the end of the south east searched channel, about 2200 B/3 and 2200 B/4 respectively.
HMS Una and HMS P 35 were to keep to the westward of 14°10'E until 0001 B/4 for Una and 0001 B/5 for HMS P 35. Submarines encountered south of 36°30'N must not be attacked. (7)
4 Jan 1942 Around 0830 B/4, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. (6)
11 Jan 1942 Around 1530 B/11, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet.
On departure exercises were carried out with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) which departed around the same time. (8)
11 Jan 1942 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 3rd war patrol see the map below.

13 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1137 B/13, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) that an aircraft had reported six medium sized merchant vessels escorted by three destroyers to the south-south-east of Kelibia, Tunisia in position 36°40'N, 11°12'E on course 180° at 9 knots.

[We have been unable to place an Italian convoy in this position at this time. Also no Italian destroyers or torpedo boats were this area on that day. Vichy French phosphate ships usually travelled singly and unescorted. The only Vichy French warship in the area was the torpedo boat L'Iphigénie leaving Sfax on 12 January and arriving at Bizerte on 13 January. (7)
13 Jan 1942 At 2155 B/13, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) left her current patrol area to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2001 B/12. (8)

14 Jan 1942 At 0525 B/14, north-north-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°57'N, 12°24'E, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) sighted an object ahead and dived. The object turned out to be a patrol vessel thought to be a (motor) torpedo boat which later withdrew towards Lampedusa.

The only movements in this area were were able to find information on were the following; The Italian motor torpedo boat (MAS-boat) MAS 560 arrived at Pantelleria from Lampedusa at 1000 B/14 and the naval auxiliary Epomeo (243 GRT, built 1929) arrived at Pantelleria from Lampedusa at 1130 B/14. (8)

16 Jan 1942 At 1422 B/16, well to the south-east of Cape Spartivento, in position 37°37'N, 16°50'E, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) sighted a convoy, hull down, on bearing 170°. She closed at full speed but made no impression as the range was about 10 to 11 miles and did not decrease. The convoy was making for Messina.
[We were unable find an Axis convoy or even a single merchant ship, hospital ship or escort vessel in this area at this time.] (8)

20 Jan 1942 From 1400 B/20, when to the east-north-east of Punta Alice, in position 39°27'N, 17°22'E, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) stalked an A/S schooner for two and a half hours before giving up any attempt for an attack.
At 1900 B/20, HMS Una left patrol to return to Malta. (8)
23 Jan 1942 Around 0930 B/23, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)

9 Feb 1942 Around 1915 B/9, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto and to form a patrol line (3 - 39°25'N, 17°20'E - 60° - 10 - 150° - 20) together with HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) and HMS Tempest (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN). HMS Una in position 'A' 39°25'N, 17°20'E, HMS Tempest in position 'B' and HMS Upright in position 'C'.
It had been intended to sail HMS Una earlier but a fuel tank leak prevented this and she had to be taken in hand for repairs.
[No log is available for this period and also the patrol report does not mention daily positions so no map can be displayed.] (8)

12 Feb 1942 HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian tanker Lucania (8106 GRT, built 1902) about 20 nautical miles north-east of Crotone, Calabria, Italy in position 39°20'N, 17°25'E.

This was most regrettable as Lucania had obtained a safe conduct from the British government as she was carrying Italian nationals from Eritrea and should not have been molested. The Italians were understandably quite angry and initiated an intense A/S search which resulted in the sinking of HMS Tempest (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN). At the time Lt. Martin had developed pneumonia and had a 104°F (40° C) temperature and did not realise the mistake he had made.
Lucania had sailed at 0848 B/12 from Taranto for Genoa.

After Lucania was torpedoed, the torpedo boat Lince and the tug Atlante sailed from Taranto at 1812 B/12 to hunt the submarine and tow the tanker respectively. Lince hunted the submarine unsuccessfully as her sonar had broken down, she then entered Crotone at 1200 B/13 and sailed from there at 1650 B/13 for Taranto where she arrived at 2100 B/13. At 0935 B/12, the transport Bosforo (3648 GRT, built 1929) escorted by the torpedo boat Circe had sailed from Messina for Taranto. At 2306 B/12, Circe received the order to take over the hunt between 39°10'N and 39°30'N and 17°40'E and 18°00'E, and Bosforo was diverted to Crotone. At 0315 B/13, Circe got an echo at 1760 metres and started the attack which resulted in the sinking of HMS Tempest.
1440 B/12 - Sighted a large southbound tanker in ballast. Started attack.
1513 B/12 - In position 39°20'N, 17°25'E fired three torpedoes from 2000 yards and obtained one hit. The tanker was seen to catch fire and was abandoned by the crew. (8)
13 Feb 1942 On board HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN), the Commanding Officer, Lt. Martin developed an ever higher temperature and the patrol had to be abandoned and course was set towards Malta. (8)

14 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1039 B/14, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) to return to Malta through position 36°20'N, 15°35'E. She was also informed that 'Force K' and a convoy may arrive Grand Harbour 0700 B/16 from by the south-east swept channel. (9)
15 Feb 1942 At 1117 A/15, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) sighted a Maestrale-class destroyer proceeding on course 050° at 20 knots.
[No position is given in the patrol report but it was probably in the area to the south of the Straits of Messina / east of Sicily.]

[The torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina at 1000 B/15 and arrived at Augusta at 1425 B/15 but she was going the opposite direction. Given the time she certainly could not have been the vessel sighted. No other destroyer is reported to have been in the area.] (8)

15 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1349 B/15, referring to his signal timed 1039 B/14, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) to pass north and west of position 36°20'N, 15°40'E owing to possible U-boat patrol to the south and east of that point.
This must have been to evade the Italian submarine Mocenigo which was on patrol in this area.
This singal was based on ULTRA (codebreaking) source [signal in DEFE3/838/2 pp. 905-906]. (9)
16 Feb 1942 Shortly after 0800 B/16, HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. Lt. Martin was admitted to hospital. (8)

16 Feb 1942 Around 1930 B/16, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) had barely been in harbour for a few hours when she sailed again around 1930 B/16 from Malta for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). Spare Commanding Officer Lt. Norman had taken over from Lt. Martin which had been admitted to hospital.

HMS Una was ordered to form a patrol line with HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) in the eastern approaches of Tripoli, to intercept an important convoy. HMS Una is to patrol in position 'Y', 32°48'N, 14°11'E. To get to this position she was to proceed through position 34°30'N, 14°11'E.

This was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).

At 0710 B/22, the Caio Duilio squadron sighted the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoy covered by the Gorizia squadron. Scirocco left the convoy as it crossed the 34th parallel and arrived at Messina at 1424 B/23. The Caio Duilio squadron turned back and arrived at Taranto at 0202 B/24-0230 B/24. The Gorizia squadron also left the convoy and returned to Messina, arriving at 1159 B/24-1340 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (10) 16 Feb 1942 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 5th war patrol see the map below. 17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (9) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1205 B/20 Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). At 2000 B/20 proceed as follows; HMS P 34 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E to Area K off Kerkenah. HMS Una through positions 33°20'N, 13°45'E and 33°20'N, 12°55'E to Area N south of 33°45'W and west of 12°55'E. HMS P 39 was to operate in area L, 32°10'N, 15°00'W - 000° - 40 - 50. Three more submarines will sail shortly to concentrate again with HMS Una and HMS P 39 in the vicinity of Khoms. In a signal timed 2207 B/20 they were ordered by Capt. S.10 to return to their previous patrol positions by 0001 B/23. (9) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow. The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B). Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy. To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys. At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona). At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron. At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay. At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23. At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22. At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24. At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy. Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23. Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23. The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24. The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (9) 21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (9) 22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots. In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots. The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (9) 23 Feb 1942 While searching for the reported enemy convoy, depth charges were dropped near HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) between 1000 B/23 and 1130 B/23. In all about 70 were dropped with a range varying between 200 yards and 3 nautical miles. No damage was caused except for a few broken lights. Earlier that day, at 0920 B/23, a small diesel trawler had been sighted. The enemy passed one mile to the northward of the submarine. Enemy course was 230°. All this was near Una's patrol position which was 32°48'N, 14°11'E. During the time HMS Una was kept down it appeared that the convoy passed about 5 miles to the northward of her. At 1200 B/23, two destroyer were seen to return to the area at high speed but they did not drop depth charges. At 1238 B/23, a destroyer passed astern of HMS Una at close range while proceeding to the eastward at over 30 knots. At 2057 B/23, HMS Una which already had retired a bit to the northwards left patrol to return to Malta. (8) 23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E. HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E. They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (9) 25 Feb 1942 At 0730 A/25, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8) 5 Mar 1942 At 1215 B/5, while at Malta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was straddled by two heavy calibre bombs. One landed in the creek about 150 yards on the starboard beam and the other landed on the shore about 200 yards on the port beam. No apparent damage was caused. (11) 6 Mar 1942 At 1205 B/5, while at Malta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) the submarine base was attacked once more and a stick of bombs exploded near the submarine moorings. A fuel lighter was hit and exploded. HMS Una was not damaged though. (11) 7 Mar 1942 At 2200 B/7, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol near Kerkennah no.4 buoy until A.M. on 10 March and then in area 'K'. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (8) 9 Mar 1942 At 2102 B/9, on surfacing in position 34°45'N, 11°58'E, after a day of submerged patrol, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) set course for area 'K' between Mahedia, Lampedusa and Kerkennah. At 2120 B/9, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1810 B/9 was received. Course was set to try to intercept a 14 knot convoy around 0700 B/10. (12) 9 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1745 B/9, the Vice Admiral Malta informed HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, ORP) of the following; Force K will sweep north from position 36°05'N, 11°50'E between 0200 B/10 and 0300 B/10. In a follow up signal the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to these three submarines; My 1745 B/9. P 31 patrol in position 35°27'N, 12°23'E from 0400 B/10 to intercept a 14 knot ship on approximate course 140° then return to arrive Grand Harbour. Sokol keep west of 11°40'E until 0400 B/10 then return to northern route. Una remain on that route south of 35°30'N. [The 14-knot ship referred to was the 14-knot transport Luciano Manara (8396 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce which sailed from Trapani at 1600 B/9-1700 B/9. Shortly after leaving Trapani, Centauro had defects which forced her to return to Trapani at 1730 B/9. From 2125 B/9, the convoy came under air attack and Luciano Manara was damaged by RAF torpedo bombers (three Swordfish and three Albacores, one Albacore cashed on landing on return to Malta) at 2310 B/9. She dropped anchor at Pantelleria at 0053 B/10 and then sailed again at 0950 B/10 to return to Palermo where she arrived at 0135 B/11. (ULTRA source, see DEFE3/939/2, pp. 1091-1092)] [Force K, made up of the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. M.S. Slattery, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyer HMS Kingston (Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta around 2000 B/9 to intercept the above convoy. As the convoy broke off its passage they failed to find it and they returned to Malta around 0900 B/10.] (13) 10 Mar 1942 While attempting to attack an northbound escorted merchant ship HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is detected and depth charged by one of the escorts. 0548 B/10 - Dived in position 35°12'N, 11°50'E which was the anticipated 0700 B/10 position of the expected 14 knot southbound convoy. [The submarine had been informed by S.10's signal timed 1810 B/9 of a 14-knot convoy and ordered to intercept it at 0700/10 in 35°19'N, 11°50'E (ULTRA source, see DEFE3/939/2, pp. 1091-1092). This was the 14-knot transport Luciano Manara (8396 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce which sailed from Trapani at 1600 B/9-1700 B/9. Shortly after leaving Trapani, Centauro had defects which forced her to return to Trapani at 1730 B/9. From 2125 B/9, the convoy came under air attack and Luciano Manara was damaged by RAF torpedo bombers at 2310 B/9. She dropped anchor at Pantelleria at 0053 B/10 and then sailed again at 0950 B/10 to return to Palermo where she arrived at 0135 B/11.] 0830 B/10 - No sign of the southbound convoy [see above]. Set course to intercept a 11 knot northbound convoy in its anticipated 1100 B/10 position. 1015 B/10 - In the anticipated interception position, 35°22'N, 11°40'E. 1130 B/10 - An aircraft was sighted to the westward. 1138 B/10 - Heard HE bearing 225°. 1140 B/10 - Near position 35°24'N, 11°39'E sighted a medium sized merchant vessel bearing 225°. Range was 5000 yards. Enemy course 005°. Started attack. 1145 B/10 - The target was seen to be escorted by two destroyers and an armed trawler. Also two aircraft were overhead. 1152 B/10 - The target zigged towards placing Una in a very favourable attack position. 1154 B/10 - The target was seen to turn away and the trawler was seen to hoist a red flag and turned towards. 1155 B/10 - The trawler was still approaching and Lt. Norman was forced to break off the attack as Una had been detected. Una now went deep. 1159 B/10 - The counter attack started. 1233 B/10 - Between 1159 and 1233 hours 16 depth charges were dropped but none were close. Una meanwhile withdraw to the south. This convoy was most likely the one made up of the German transport Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the destroyer Bersagliere, the torpedo boat Castore and the tug Instancabile (former Yugoslav salvage vessel Spasilac, 740 tons, built 1929, captured in 1941). They sailed from Tripoli at 1900 B/9-2100 B/9 and arrived at Naples at 1725 B/11, except Instancabile which went to Lampedusa (see below) and Bersagliere which had been detached to Palermo. At 1157 B/10, the escorting aircraft gave a submarine alarm and Bersagliere went on to attack with eight depth charges and ordered Castore to hunt the submarine. The torpedo boat dropped eight depth charges but was getting echoes from several directions; then, in a second run, dropped another four depth charges. At 1300 B/10, Castore abandoned the hunt and rejoined the convoy. At 1305 B/10, the aircraft gave a new alarm and Bersagliere dropped seven depth charges (this must have been a bogus sighting). In both instances, the destroyer and the torpedo boat did not detect the submarine and thus attacked haphazardly. Unfortunately, Bersagliere report does not give either positions, but at 0810 B/10, the convoy was in view of Kerkennah buoy No.4 so the convoy must have been reasonably near HMS Una three hours later. (8) 12 Mar 1942 At 0830 B/12, while patrolling near Mahdia, Tunisia in approximate position 35°33'N, 11°13'E, a Vichy-French patrol vessel, similar to the British PC 74, was seen proceeding southwards just inside territorial waters. The French flag was painted on the ships side. (8) 13 Mar 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sank the Italian schooner Maria Immacolata (248 GRT, cargo of sugar and general cargo bound for Tripoli) with gunfire 7.8 nautical miles bearing 135° off Mahedia, Tunisia. A second schooner, Maria Camali, was in company at a distance but was not seen by the submarine. 1013 B/13 - Sighted two fishing boats to the northward. 1140 B/13 - Sighted a 3-masted schooner bearing 040°, range 6000 yards, enemy course 130°. 1145 B/13 - Gave chase to this schooner. 1218 B/13 - Surfaced in position 35°26'N, 11°11'E, 60° on the Port bow of the schooner at a range of 600 yards. Engaged with the gun but it misfired. 1222 B/13 - The schooner turned away towards the coast. Gave chase and engaged it with the Lewis gun. 1223 B/13 - The crew of the schooner abandoned ship after firing one round with a rifle. The defect on the 12pdr. gun was made good and fire was opened from 80 yards. 1243 B/13 - The schooner sank in position 135°, Mahedia Light, 7.8 nautical miles. Closed the survivors in their boat. They were not picked up and ordered to make for the coast 8 nautical miles away. 1255 B/13 - Dived and withdrew to the east. During the action 30 round were fired with the 12pdr. gun and 500 with the Lewis gun. (8) 14 Mar 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) attacked an escorted transport in approximate position 35°29'N, 12°15'E (west of Lampedusa) with three torpedoes which were fired from long range. No hits were obtained. This was the German transport Trapani (1855 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cantore on passage from Tripoli to Trapani. The transport sighted four torpedo tracks (only three had been fired) and took evasive action while a Savoia 79 bomber dropped a green smoke float to mark the position of the submarine. Cantore rushed to the position and dropped ten depth charges, the first two set at 25 meters and the remainder at 50 meters charges (in Una only eight explosions were heard so probably two failed to explode). By precaution, Trapani was directed to Pantelleria before resuming her passage on the 15th. 1152 B/14 - Went deep as it appeared a systematic air seach was going on in the area. 1203 B/14 - Heard HE on bearing 280°. It was thought this was a patrol vessel out of Lampedusa. 1205 B/14 - Deciphered Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 B/10 (1 merchant ship, 1 destroyer in position 165° - Lampione Island - 36 miles on course 350°, 6 knots at 0954 B/14) and realised that the HE was a convoy that was reported in this signal. Came to periscope depth and turned towards. 1213 B/14 - At periscope depth. Sighted a transport of about 4000 tons and a destroyer bearing 285° at a range of 5000 to 6000 yards. Enemy course was 015°. Closed on a 115° track. 1223 B/14 - In position 190° - Lampione Island - 7 miles fired three torpedoes at the transport from a range of 5000 to 6000 yards. 1227 B/14 - Heard a loud explosion. This may have been either a bomb exploding or a torpedo hit at a running range of 6000 yards. 1232 B/14 - A counter attack was carried out by the destroyer which dropped eight single depth charges. 1330 B/14 - HE of the destroyer faded out. (12) 14 Mar 1942 At 1400 B/14, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) set course to the eastward in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1051 B/13 which ordered her to patrol in position 37°43'N, 15°33'E (South of the Straits of Messina) as of dawn on the 17th proceeding through position 35°20'N, 12°35'E at 2000 B/14 and then through position 36°06'N, 14°03'E. (12) 16 Mar 1942 At 0621 B/16, near Cape Passaro, Sicily, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) picked up HE (diesel) on bearing 290°. Una turned towards. Periscope visibility was 2000 to 3000 yards. Nothing was sighted. At 0625 B/16, there was still nothing in sight. At 0633 B/16, the HE faded out. Periscope visibility was 4000 yards at that moment. It is thought this may have been a submarine or motor torpedo boat returning to Augusta. We have been unable to place an enemy submarine in this area at this time but the German motor torpedo boats (E-boats) S-54, S-31, S-35, S-36 and S-61 passed through this area between 0600 B/16 - 0630 B/16 on their return to Augusta (arrived around 0745 B/16) from a minelaying operation off Malta. (8) 17 Mar 1942 At 2040 B/17, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) picked up HE thought to be of a destroyer / torpedo boat on bearing 020°. At 2105 B/17, the HE faded out on bearing 070°. Nothing had been seen of the source of the HE so HMS Una surfaced in position 37°40'N, 15°35'E. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (8) 18 Mar 1942 At 2105 B/18, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0520 B/18. Course was set to comply. She was now to patrol on a line 060°-240° between position M and N. (37°43'N, 15°33'E and 37°47.N, 15°45'E respectively). (8) 19 Mar 1942 At 2055 B/19, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced in position 37°40'N, 15°31'E. Course was now set to leave patrol and return to Malta. She was to proceed through position 37°00'N, 15°36'E to arrive off Malta at 0645 B/21. (8) 21 Mar 1942 At 1035 B/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8) 31 Mar 1942 Around 2130 B/31, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol South of the Strait of Messina. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (8) 2 Apr 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) arrived in her patrol area between Cape Spartivento and Cape Bruzzano. (8) 4 Apr 1942 At 1300 C/4, east-south-east of Cape Spartivento, in approximate position 37°54'N, 16°10'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted two minesweepers proceeding eastwards along the coast. Half an hour later they turned back to the westwards. (8) 5 Apr 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Ninetto G. (5355 GRT, built 1913) about 12 nautical miles south of Capo dell'Armi in position 37°45'N, 15°41'E. The above mentioned Ninetto G. was in convoy together with the transport Veloce (Italian, 5437 GRT, built 1911). They were proceeding from Taranto to Messina escorted by auxiliary Morosini and the auxiliary submarine chaser Ciprus. Following the sinking the torpedo boat Orsa equipped with the German S-Gerät (sonar) was ordered to the scene and hunted the submarine in the evening, dropping two pattern of nine depth charges, followed by two more. A large oil slick was observed and the submarine was believed destroyed. Orsa remained on the scene until the next afternoon when she dropped two more depth charges before leaving the scene on the morning of the 7th as she was relieved by the torpedo boat Circe. Ninetto G. had a crew of thirty. There were twenty-eight survivors (three of them were wounded). Two of the crew were missing. 0555 C/5 - Heard HE bearing 090°. 0559 C/5 - Sighted a destroyer bearing 085° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 300°. 0600 C/5 - Dived in position 37°43'N, 15°54'E, as Una was up moon from the enemy. The destroyer could not be seen through the periscope. 0618 C/5 - HE faded. This was probably either the destroyer Antonio da Noli or Emanuele Pessagno which sailed from Tripoli respectively at 0930 B/4 and 0852 B/4 and arrived at Messina at 0730 B/5 and 0740 B/5. --------------------------------- 1140 C/5 - Sighed smoke bearing 040°. 1155 C/5 - Sighted two merchant ships in single line ahead. Started attack. 1210 C/5 - Sighted that the merchant vessels were escorted by two yachts. 1254 C/5 - In position 37°45'N, 15°41'E fired four torpedoes at the 2nd ship of the line from 3000 yards. This ship was thought o be a merchant ship of 7000-8000 tons. Two hits were obtained. 1304 C/5 - The ship was heard to sink. 1315 C/5 - The counter attack started. Una meanwhile withdrew to the souh-east. 1318 C/5 - A depth charge exploded quite close causing some minor damage. 1330 C/5 - The counter attack ended. In all 14 depth charges were dropped. Between 1345 C/5 and 1420 C/5 - Six more depth charges were dropped some distance away. Between 2100 C/5 and 2400 C/5 - About sixty depth charges in patterns of six were dropped at intervals to the north-westward in the vicinity of the attack. 2230 C/5 - Una surfaced in position 37°35'N, 16°08'E. Depth charging was going on about 15 miles away. Commenced to withdraw to the eastward. (8) 7 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1131 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN); HMS Una was to patrol in position 37°42'N, 15°37'E from 2301 B/7 to 0531 B/8. HMS P 34 was to patrol in position 37°00'N, 16°14'E from 0259 B/8 to 0731 B/8. In a follow up signal timed 2135 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed both submarines that Wellington aircraft from Malta will search and attack enemy shipping after midnight. This was to try to intercept the BETA-convoy which was made up of the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare which sailed from Naples at 0130 B/7-0208 B/7. The Italian torpedo boat Orsa had sailed from Messina at 1745 B/5 for an A/S sweep and attacked a submarine at 2010 B/5 in position 200° - Cape Spartivento - 3 miles and claimed it sunk. She was then ordered to reinforce the escort of the Ankara. At 0415 B/7, the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza departed from Messina to join Ankara. At 0800 B/7, the Italian torpedo boat Lince sailed from Messina to reinforce the escort and joined at 1301 B/7, followed by Orsa at 1336 B/7 and shortly after by Giuseppe Dezza. Ankara, Antoniotto Usodimare, Giuseppe Dezza and Orsa arrived at Messina at 1728-1750 B/7. Lince and two MAS boats were on an A/S search, the torpedo boat returning to Messina only at 0300 B/8. Ankara, Antoniotto Usodimare and Lince sailed again at 2152 B/7-2330 B/7. At 0022 B/8, Ankara and Antoniotto Usodimare accidentally collided in position 37°55'20"N, 15°25'10"E. The three ships returned to Messina at 0422 B/8-0426 B/8. Ankara, escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince, sailed from Messina at 2150 B/8. At 0837 B/10, the Italian torpedo boat Pallade met them to pilot them in. They arrived at Benghazi at 1145 B/10. (14) 7 Apr 1942 At 1955 C/7, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) was to the south-south-east of Cape Spartivento, in approximate position 37°51'N, 16°06'E, when a transport escorted by a destroyer were sighted at a distance of 4-5 nautical miles. HMS Una tried to close to less than 3 miles but had to abandon the attack at 2105 C/7 when this it was apparent the range could not be closed any further. This was the Italian transport Arlesiana (5702 GRT, built 1915) escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba. They had sailed from Taranto at 8 knots at 1510 B/6 and arrived at Catania at 0120 B/8. (8) 8 Apr 1942 At 0727 C/7, south of dell'Armi, in approximate position 37°44'N, 15°46'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted the mast and funnels of two cruisers bearing 245°. Enemy course was 320° at a speed (by revs) of 25 knots. Range was 4-5 miles. HMS Una attempted to close to attack range but half an hour later the approach had to be broken off and the cruisers disappeared from sight. This were the battleship Caio Duilio and the light cruiser Muzio Attendolo screened by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Malocello, Nicolò Zeno and Ugolino Vivaldi. They had sailed from Taranto at 1600 B/7-1725 B/7. They were later reinforced by the torpedo boats Circe and Orsa which sailed from Messina at 0300 B/8-0340 B/8. They arrived at Messina at 0812 B/8-1027 B/8, except Antoniotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi, which were detached at 0812 B?8-0830 B/8 and continued on to Trapani where they arrived at 1820 B/8 while Antonio Pigafetta and Nicolò Zeno were detached to proceed to Reggio Calabria, arriving at 0900 B/8-0915 B/8. At 1430 C/8, two funnels with red cross markings were sighted bearing 020° at a range of 5 miles. At 1435 C/8, this vessel was identified as the repatriation ship Duilio (Italian, 24281 GRT, built 1923) which was expected though the ship was 48 hours ahead of schedule. Duilio had sailed from Trieste at 0900 B/6 at a speed of 16.5 knots and arrived at Gibraltar on 11 April to pick up Italian nationals from East Africa which were to be repatriated. (8) 8 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1046 B/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was ordered to remain on patrol as in Capt. S.10's singal timed 1131 B/7 until A.M. on the 9th. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was ordered to proceed direct to the area off Cape Santa Maria di Leuca off the Gulf of Taranto. (14) 8 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1914 B/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was informed that she was reported by enemy aircraft at 1846 B/8. (14) 10 Apr 1942 At 0645 C/10, south of the Straits of Messina, in approximate position 37°42'N, 15°33'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted a destroyer crossing her bow at a range of 800 yards. Enemy course was 110° at 29 knots. We have been unable to find a destroyer/torpedo boat in this area at that time/date. At 1135 C/10, on returning to periscope depth in approximate position 37°40'N, 15°53'E, sighted a 4000 ton tanker and escort bearing 080° at a range of only 700 yards. No HE was picked up. Turned hard to starboard at 1138 C/10 and went to 50 feet to avoid being run down and also to attempt to turn and fire at close range on a 170° track. [No course and speed of the enemy given in the patrol report or logbook.] At 1148 C/10, HMS Una was on a 175° track. Range was now about 3500 yards and the attack was broken off. It was later discovered that the Asdic was defective. This were most likely the Italian naval tanker Marte< (2502 GRT, built 1892) escorted by the destroyer Turbine which had sailed from Taranto at 9,5 knots at 0810 B/9-0900 B/9. The auxiliary Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940) had sailed from Messina at 0210 B/10 to reinforce the escort. They arrived at Messina at 1500 B/10-1530 B/10. (8) 11 Apr 1942 At 0040 C/11, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1349 B/10 which ordered her to leave patrol through positions 36°25'N, 15°27'E and 36°05'N, 14°50'E to arrive off Marsamxett Harbour P.M. on the 12th. Course was set for a position to the south-east of Augusta with a view to intercept enemy submarine arriving or leaving at dawn. At 0620 C/11, HMS Una dived in position 37°03'N, 15.40'E. At 1245 C/11, HMS Una was in position 130° - Cape Murro di Proco - 5 miles. No enemy submarines had been encountered so course was set to the southward. (8) 12 Apr 1942 At 1345 B/12, a rather close heavy bomb explosion was felt by HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN). Around 1955 B/12, HMS Una ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8) 18 Apr 1942 At 1704 B/18, while laying on he bottom of the harbour at Malta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is near missed by a bomb causing many minor defects, most could by repaired quickly. (15) 19 Apr 1942 Around 0450 B/19, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'T' to the west of Tripoli. For the daily positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (8) 20 Apr 1942 At 1405 B/20, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1053 B/20, which stated the following; Ref. sailing orders cancel area T. Proceed through position 35°40'N, 12°50'E to Area J, west of 11°55'E between 35°35'N. and 36°05'N. Course was set to comply with these orders. (12) 21 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1025 B/21, the Capt. S.10's sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Adjust patrols to be in vicinity line 000° through 35°40'N, 11°49'E after noon 23/4. Further orders will follow. (14) 21 Apr 1942 At 2130 B/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) surfaced in position 36°08'N, 11°49'E and set course to the westward to patrol to the north of Kuriat the following day. (8) 22 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1031 B/22, referring to his signal timed 1025 B/21, the Capt. S. 10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) that two 14-knot escorted merchant ships may be encountered proceeding north on meridian 11°49'E crossing 34°30'N about 1400 B/23. At 2030 B/23, HMS Una was extend her patrol area east to 12°50'E and north to 36°10'N. This must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) en-route from Tripoli (departed 1935 B/22-2040 B/22) at 14.5 knots for Naples escorted by the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Centauro and Pallade. At about 1630 B/23-1747 B/23, about 30 miles north of Pantelleria, there was a change of escort with another convoy proceeding to Tripoli: This convoy was made up of the transports Gino Allegri (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1941) and Agostino Bertani (Italian, 8329 GRT, built 1941) and had sailed from Naples at 2245 B/22-2330 B/22, proceeding at 15.5 knots, escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and the Italian torpedo boat Cantore. The information was once again based on an ULTRA source (see DEFE3/753/486, frame 490 and DEFE3/754, frames 8, 78). (14) 22 Apr 1942 At 2120 B/22, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced in position 35°54'N, 11°07'E and set course to the eastward to comply with the orders in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1025 B/21. (8) 23 Apr 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) patrolled in the vicinity of position 35°42'N, 11°49'E during this day but nothing was sighted except aircraft patrols P.M. The expected northbound convoy was not sighted. (8) 25 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1232 B/25 the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN); Reconnaissance suggests two important 14 knots southbound escorted ships approaching position 35°46'N, 12°09'E on approximate course 165° about 0700 B/27. Adjust patrol according to moon conditions to intercept before that time on line 345° from position given as far as 36°30'N. Other traffic may be encountered later in vicinity and may pass between Lampion and Lampdeusa. ['Reconnaissance suggests' in this case actually means information from 'ULTRA', codebreaking of enemy signals. The convoy was not yet at sea at this moment see file DEFE3/754, frame 82.] This signal referred to the convoy made up of the transports Monreale (Italian, 5323 GRT, built 1941) and Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) proceeding from Naples (departure around 0240 B/26-0330 B/26) to Tripoli. On departure from Naples they were escorted by the Italian destroyers Premuda, Lanzerotto Malocello. At 1125 B/26, the Italian torpedo boat Castore sailed from Palermo to reinforce the escort near position 38°22'N, 12°09.5'E. At 1635 B/26, Unione’s steering gear broke down and she was detached to Palermo escorted by Castore they arrived at 2322 B/26. The remaining ships arrived at Tripoli at 2031 B/27-2055 B/27. (14) 26 Apr 1942 At 1200 B/26, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) which is patrolling off Ras Dimass, Tunisia, sets course to the north-east to comply with the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1232 B/25 and 1005 B/26. This last signal stated the following; My 1232 B/25. Alternative route appears to be a course of 185° to arrive in position 36°01'N, 11°49'E about 0530 B/27. Patrol vicinity of position 36°20'N, 11°55'E until dawn 27/4. Anticipate enemy pass that position 0400 B to westwards or 0430 B to eastwards. Do not approach Pantellaria closer than 20 miles and retire if possible to S.S.E. on leaving patrol position in search of other targets. (16) 27 Apr 1942 At 0300 B/27, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived in position 36°20'N, 11°55'E (south of Pantelleria) to intercept the southbound convoy. There was a bright moon and the sea was calm. At 0410 B/27, flares were dropped and enemy AA fire was sighted bearing 125° approximately 12 miles away in approximate position 36°08'N, 12°08'E. Una set course to close. At 0424 B/27, an underwater explosion was heard. At 0530 B/27, HMS Una dived in position 36°10'N, 12°01'E. At 0530 B/27, HMS Una arrived in the position of the aircraft attack on the convoy. Nothing was sighted and course was set towards Lampione Island. At 1308 B/27, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1001 B/27 was received which stated the following; Patrol vicinity of position 36°09'N, 11°51'E from 0001 B/28 in anticipation of a northbound transport at about 0430 B on course 005°. Aircraft may attack, before midnight. Leave position not later than 0800 B/28 and return to Malta through positions 35°42'N, 12°50'E and 36°11'N, 14°21'E to enter Marsamxett at 2030 B/30. Course was set to the north-west to comply with the orders in this signal. This signal referred to the German transport Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) and the Italian transport Reginaldo Giuliani (6837 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Pallade and Centauro. They had sailed from Tripoli at 1137 B/27-1300 B/27. At 0634 B/28, off Pantelleria, Folgore and Centauro left the convoy to reinforce the escort of a southbound convoy made up of the Italian tanker Saturno (5022 GRT, built 1914) and the Italian transport San Luigi (4356 GRT, built 1905) escorted by the torpedo boats Cigno, Castore and Lince which had sailed from Palermo at 0955 B/27-1114 B/27 for Tripoli. Cigno left this convoy to join Pallade, escorting Reichenfels and Reginaldo Giuliani to Naples. The bad weather forced Reginaldo Giuliani and Pallade to stop at Trapani at 1430 B/28-0930 B/29. They arrived at Naples at 1315 B/30. Reichenfels escorted by Cigno arrived at Palermo at 2027 B/29. In the meantime, the Saturno convoy continued to Tripoli but, at 0831 B/28, Lince indicated that she had seakeeping difficulties and was detached to Pantelleria. At 0905 B/28, San Luigi could not make more than 6 knots in the heavy seas, and Centauro was ordered to escort her to Pantelleria and then rejoin the convoy. However, at 1035 B/28, Saturno and her escorts also turned back to take refuge in Pantelleria as the intense scirocco made the proceedings quite difficult. At 0202 B/29, Saturno escorted by Folgore, Castore, Centauro (Lince remained in Pantelleria for temporary repairs) sailed from Pantelleria. At 0820 B/30, they were met by the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari which piloted them to Tripoli where they arrived at 1138 B/30-1225 B/30. (16) 28 Apr 1942 At 0805 B/28, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0504 B/28, which stated the following; Southbound tanker damaged by aircraft in position 37°17'N, 11°42'E at 0045 B/28 may be proceeding at less than 12 knots. Remain last patrol position ordered until 2030 B/28 then return to Malta as previously ordered. Course was set to proceed to position 36°10'N, 11°50'E to intercept this damaged southbound tanker. At 0900 B/28, HMS Una was back in position but no damaged tanker was encountered. This signal must have referred to the tanker Saturno (Italian, 5029 GRT, built 1914) which was in convoy with the transport San Luigi (4419 GRT, built 1905). They were on passage from Palermo to Tripoli escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Cigno, Castore and Lince. Due to the bad weather contitions encountered the convoy returned to Pantelleria to continue it's passage at a later date. Due to the convoy returning it was possibly thought the tanker was damaged [See also the event for 27 April 1942.] The convoy was attacked by a single torpedo bomber (Albacore) at 0048 B/28 20 miles north of Pantelleria but suffered no damage. The torpedo boats made smoke to shield the convoy and San Luigi fired a burst of machine gun fire. (16) 30 Apr 1942 At 2035 B/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8) 4 May 1942 Around 0430 B/4, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for Alexandria. She was to leave Malta by the north-east searched channel (71° from St. Elmo Light for 18 miles) and then through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E around 0001 B/5 and through position 34°50'N, 20°00'E about 1400 B/7. Further instructions would be signalled by Capt. S.1. Due to the heavy bombing of Malta the 10th Submarine Fotilla was evacuated from Malta to Alexandria. Una carried 14 passengers and stores. [No log is available so no map can be displayed.] (17) 5 May 1942 In a signal timed 1810 C/5, Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) to proceed through position 32°23'N, 28°46'N to arrive at the entrance to the Alexandria searched channel at 0530 C/13. (18) 13 May 1942 Around 0745 C/13, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived at Alexandria. [It is likely that during May 1942 HMS Una was docked but no log of this month is available so we can not be sure.] (17) 31 May 1942 Around 1345 C/31, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) left Alexandria for exercises. She returned to harbour around 0650 C/1. The exercises had included night exercises. 6 Jun 1942 Around 1730 C/6, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Alexandria for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to give cover during convoy operations to Malta and took position 'A' in 35°37'N, 18°44'E, the southernmost point of a patrol line (bearing 050° at 12 miles intervals) with HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) in position 'B', HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) in position 'D'. The patrol line was to cover the convoy proceeding to Malta from Alexnadria during Operation VIGOROUS). For the daily positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (17) 13 Jun 1942 At 0500 C/13, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) arrived in patrol position 'A' (35°37'N, 18°44'E). At 0730 C/13, an Italian hospital ship passed 1500 yards off track. Enemy course was 350° at 12 knots. This was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926). She had sailed from Derna at 1100 B/12 and arrived at Naples at 1300 B/14. (17) 15 Jun 1942 At 0630 C/15, while still in position 'A' (35°37'N, 18°44'E), HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) heard a heavy explosion to the north-east. At 0730 C/15, a large column of black smoke was seen on bearing 050° at an estimated range of about 15 to 20 miles. HMS Una set course to proceed towards at speed. At 0830 C/15, sighted that a white smoke screen was being laid to the north of the column of smoke. The range did not seem to be closing. At 0900 C/15, the smoke was dwindling. Still there was no ship in sight so set course to return to the patrol position. (17) 17 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1833 C/17, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. (20) 21 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2135 C/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Area 33°00'N, 47°00'E, 23°14'E - 310° - 65 miles is not to be crossed during night 22nd/23rd. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) pass to southward of area. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passing to the south and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to the north of Gavdo Island. (20) 23 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1117 C/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Proceed to following positions from whence you are expected end of searched channel at the approx. time given but submarines are not to arrive off the boom after 2030 hours or before 0730 hours. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) through position 32°19'N, 29°08'E to arrive at (the end of the searched channel) 1401 C/27. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) through position 31°39'N, 29°02'E to arrive at 0459 C/28. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) through position 31°53'N, 28°55'E to arrive at 0801 C/28. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) through position 32°01'N, 28°59'E to arrive at 0501 C/29. (20) 28 Jun 1942 Around 0730 C/28, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Alexandria. (17) 29 Jun 1942 Around 2045 C/29, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Alexandria for Port Said. (21) 30 Jun 1942 Around 1755 C/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived at Port Said from Alexandria. (21) 3 Jul 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is docked in the floating dock at Port Said after the previous day an attempt to put her on the slipway had failed. (22) 5 Jul 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is undocked. (22) 8 Jul 1942 Around 0555 C/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Port Said for Haifa. (22) 10 Jul 1942 Around 0945 C/10, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived at Haifa from Port Said. (22) 21 Jul 1942 Around 2000 C/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Haifa for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to proceed to Malta and to patrol North of Crete en-route. For the daily positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (22) 28 Jul 1942 At 0515 C/28, when 12 miles north of Suda Bay, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 275° at a range of 5 (?, difficult to read in the patrol report) miles coming towards at speed. HMS Una crash dived to 80 feet. At 0535 C/28, the HE of the destroyer faded out to the southward. This was most likely the Italian destroyer Bersagliere which arrived at Suda Bay at 0710 B/28 (note the one hour time zone difference). Bersagliere had sailed from Navarino at 1945 B/27. At 0645 C/28, a 'Generali-class' torpedo boat and a large armed yacht were seen patrolling three miles to the northward. No Asdic transmissions were heard. HMS Una continued her passage westwards towards the Anti-Kithera Channel. This were probably the armed yacht Rovigno (451 GRT, built 1941). She was on minesweeping duty with the minesweeper RD 9 and they returned to Suda at 1235 B/28. As for the 'Generali-class torpedo boat', there were no units of that class in this area. The torpedo boats Castore, Sagittario, Lince and Lupo were at Suda on that day but did not sortie. All did not even closely resemble a 'Generali-class torpedo boat'. The destroyer Quintino Sella and the torpedo boat Lira had sailed from Leros at 1955 B/27-2000 B/27 and arrived at Suda at 0810 B-0817 B/28. It is possible that Una sighted one of them. Unless she mistook the minesweeper RD 9 for a torpedo boat. (23) 2 Aug 1942 Around 1215-1230 B/2, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta while HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. They were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1000 B/2. (17) 9 Aug 1942 Around 1100 B/9, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to carry out a special operation and to patrol to the east of Sicily. She, and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), were swept out to the end of the swept channel by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (24) 9 Aug 1942 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 11th war patrol see the map below. 11 Aug 1942 During the night of 11/12 August 1942, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) carried out special operation 'Why Not'. A group of six men were landed near Catania, Sicily, Italy to attack the local airfield. It was intended to land nine men from 'M section' of the Special Boat Squadron but one of the Folbots used in the landing was damaged by the heavy swell and three men returned to Una and the damaged Folbot was sunk. HMS Una waited for the landing party to return but they failed to show up. They hadd found the airfield, which housed JU-88's but were detected by Italian patrols and shots were fired after which they returned to the beach and went out to sea. They however missed the rendezvous with the submarine. They remained at sea in their Folbots but also failed to show up at the second rendezvous. Their Folbots were destroyed by bad weather and they ended up in the water clinging to the wreckage. Eventually they were picked up by Italian fishermen which handed them over the Italian Army. 'M section' was made up of the following men; Captain G.E.A. Duncan, Black Watch, Lieutenant E. Newby, Black Watch, Lieutenant H.A.D. Buchanan, Grenadier Guards Sergeant W. Dunbar, Argyll & Southerland Highlanders Corporal J.H. Booth, Seaforth Higlanders Corporal H. Butler, Prince of Wales Volunteers Guardman W.P. Duffey, Coldstream Guards Bombardier J. Brittlebank, Royal Artillery Marine R.R. Didcot, Royal Marines Six Folbots had been embarked for the operation, four to be used in the landing and two spare ones for re-embarkation if necessary. Also explosives had been embarked. According to Italian sources, the three officers and two ratings were captured by Italian fishermen south of the mouth of the Simeto River. They had attempted to force the fishermen to take them to Malta. They did not oppose any resistance. A sixth men was captured by two Custom guards near the river. They had been armed with pistols and hand grenades. On the morning of the 13th, 60 mines and explosives were found abandoned in San Giuseppe Arena (Catania). Four explosives had caused slight damage tot he base of a pylon of electric low tension. 1934 B/11 - Una bottomed in 14 fathoms in position 150° - Catania Breakwater Light - 2.5 miles. Having made a periscope reconnaissance of the landing beach earlier. 2120 B/11 - Surfaced. 2130 B/11 - Placed the Folbots on the casing. It was just possible to open the fore hatch at full buoyancy without shipping serious quantities of water. 2140 B/11 - Proceeded slowly inshore trimmed down. 2208 B/11 - In estimated position 1500 yards - 090° from the landing position opposite San Giuseppe Church. Disembarked the Folbots. The first Folbot was caught by an unusually heavy sea which badly damaged the Folbot and it had to be sunk. The crew and the stores could be taken off. The remaining three Folbots were launches successfully. It was decided by Captain Duncan to leave Booth, Brittlebank and Didcot in Una. 2235 B/11 - The party was last seen making good headway towards the shore. Una withdrew to the seaward. 2300 B/11 - No sign of activity ashore. It was presumed the party had made a successful landing. 0015 B/12 - Dived and proceeded inshore. 0102 B/12 - Bottomed in 11 fathoms in position 090°, 1.8 miles from the mouth of the Simeto river. 0300 B/12 - Surfaced and adjusted position to keep between 1500 and 2500 yards off the river mouth which could just be seen. An Allied air raid was in progress over Catania and heavy AA fire was observed. All ground lights were extinguished. 0315 B/12 - The air raid was completed. 0415 B/12 - There had been no sign of the landing party. No explosions were seen or heard from the direction of the aerodrome. From 0350 B/12, Una had been flashing 'S' to seaward and with caution to landward, all to no avail. 0417 B/12 - HE was heard to the seaward. 0425 B/12 - More HE was heard to the northward. It was now 10 minutes after the last scheduled time of embarkation. It was also getting light and there seemed a possibility of a search being made to seaward. 0427 B/12 - Dived and proceeded to seaward. It did not appear that an alarm had been raised ashore although the presence of surface craft pointed to the possibility that the alarm had been raided.
