It was consided that either the party had been taken prisoner or they had been delayed and abandoned the re-embarkation until a second rendezvous and had managed to partially achieve their object. 2053 B/12 - Una was now 20 miles offshore. A much needed battery charge was started. 0515 B/13 - Surfaced in position 37.25'N, 15.31'E and proceeded towards the southern end of Catania Bay. 1730 B/13 - Bottomed in 10 farthoms in position 050° - Torre del Agnone - 1.5 miles. 0030 B/14 - Surfaced and closed the landing beach. 0045 B/14 - In position 1500 yards from the pre-arranged point 090°, one mile from Torre del Agnone. Three minutes before Capt. S.10's signal timed 1912 B/13 was received which after decoding contained the following orders; Cancel operation. Retire immediately. Report when safe to do so. 0120 B/14 - Withdrew to seaward. No signals or signs of activity had been seen on the shore. 0145 B/14 - Received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1318 B/13. [We have been unable to locate what this signal was about.] 0450 B/14 - Passed Una's signal timed 0445 B/14 to Capt. S.10 which stated the following; Your 1912 B/13. Three officers Three other ranks missing. No result observed. No sign at second rendezvous. Three did not land. 0500 B/14 - Dived and proceed to the patrol area off Cape Spartivento. (17) 15 Aug 1942 At 0600 B/15, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted what was thought to be the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) proceeding on course 125° at 12 knots. At 0620 B/15, the hospital ship passed 2000 yards off track in position - 175° - Amandolea River - 10 miles. This was indeed Gradisca which had sailed from Naples at 1130 B/14 and arrived at Tobruk at 0845 B/17. (17) 15 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1043 B/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) to leave patrol through positions 37°32'N, 16°13'E, 35°23'N, 15°14'E and 35°23'N, 14°12'E to rendezvous with sweeper at 1001 B/19 at end of the swept channel. (25) 17 Aug 1942 At 0910 B/15, to the east of Augusta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted two minesweepers approaching. Una turned to the south to avoid. At 0915 B/15, these minesweepers were identified as A/S vessels. Both stopped and were listening. Range was only 1000 yards. At 0919 B/15, they both turned towards and approached. Una went deep, prepared for a depth charge attack, and turned towards to pass underneath their sterns and thence to seaward. At 0942 B/15, the HE of the enemy vessels faded. These A/S craft appeared to be diesel engined. About 200 tons, bridge aft, heavily camouflaged. No A/S transmissions were heard. They were both flying the Italian ensign. It is thought that they had been sent to the area due to Una being detected by shore hydrophones. (17) 19 Aug 1942 Around 1215 B/19, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She, and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 0930 B/19. (17) 30 Aug 1942 Around 1830 B/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol West of Crete. She was to proceed through position 35°37'N, 20°59'E en-route to her patrol area. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (2) 2 Sep 1942 At 0105 B/2, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sighted a light bearing 010°. Turned to the northward to investigate. At 0200 B/2, the light was identified as an Italian hospital ship of the Gradisca-type. Position was 35°25'N, 19°08'E. The hospital ship was proceeding to the east-south-east at 10 knots. This was the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which sailed from Naples at 1000 B/31 and arrived at Tobruk at 1800 B/3. At 1112 B/2, smoke was sighted on bearing 215°. Turned towards and closed at speed. At 1130 B/2, in position 35°23'N, 20°14'E, masts and a funnel of a merchant vessel were sighted on bearing 205° at a range of 10000 to 12000 yards. Enemy course was approximately 125°. An attack was not possible as HMS Una was already abaft the beam of the merchant vessel. No escort could be seen and given this targets position and course it was most likely another hospital ship, probably Sicilia. This was most likely the Italian hospital ship Aquileia (9448 GRT, built 1914) which sailed from Naples at 1130 B/31 and arrived at Marsa Matruh at 0930 B/4. (2) 5 Sep 1942 In a singal timed 1632 C/5, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) that an aircraft had reported that 1530 C/5 one merchant ship and one destroyer were in position 34°40'N, 23°31'E proceeding on course 350° at 7 knots. This most likely referred to the Italian tanker Giorgio (4887 GRT, built 1907) on passage from Tobruk to Suda Bay escorted by the German destroyer ZG 3 / Hermes. The Giorgio escorted by the ZG 3 / Hermes had sailed from Tobruk at 1900 B/3 and arrived at Suda at 0500 B/6. They were supposed to merge with the Italian motorship Tergestea (5890 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the torpedo boat Cassiopea which had sailed from Benghazi at 1430 B/3 and arrived at Piraeus at 0510 B/6. For some obscure reason, the two convoys did not meet (Cassiopea claimed the Giorgio convoy was not at the rendezvous at the time prescribed, perhaps an error in navigation as the two convoys ought to have met?) In a signal timed 2001 C/5, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Una to be within 20 miles of the south-west corner of her patrol area by dawn on the 7th. In a signal timed 2137 C/5 the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Una that his signal timed 2001 C/5 is to take priority over his signal timed 1632 C/5. (26) 6 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1850 C/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders; Proceed forthwith diving by day to following positions; HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), 35°48'N, 21°37'E HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), 35°15'N, 21°00'E HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), 35°05'N, 21°51'E HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) 33°33'N, 21°42'N HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN) only to be in position 32°31'N, 23°34'E by dawn September 8th. In a signal timed 2047 B/6, they were informed that three merchant ships and ten destroyers had been reported at 1740 B/6 in position 38°46'N, 20°13'E proceeding on course 170° at 10 knots. They were also informed that aircraft may attack the enemy convoy north of Navarino tonight. In a signal timed 2115 C/6, further instructions were sent by Capt. S.1. HMS P 34, HMS P 43 and HMS Una move to intercept from positions ordered in my 1850 C/6 as follows; HMS P 34, 318° - 40 miles. HMS P 43, 024° - 32 miles. HMS Una, 250° - 18 miles. Be in new positions by dawn 7th Sept. HMS Traveller, ignore my 1850 C/6 and be in position 32°45'N, 20°35'E by 0001 C/8. These submarines were to operate against an Italian convoy operation; At 0036 B/6-0150 B/6, convoy 'N', consisting of the transports Sestriere (Italian 7991 GRT, built 1942) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere (escort leader), Lampo, Legionario (equipped with new German DETE Gerät) and the Italian torpedo boats Pegaso and Partenope sailed from Brindisi. At 0012 B/6-0205 B/6, convoy 'P', consisting of transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Manara (Italian 7124 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the italian destroyers Fuciliere (escort leader), Bombardiere, Corsaro, Freccia, Camicia Nera, Geniere and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Taranto. The two convoys were to merge for defensive purposes, but then to split, this time the three Italian ships proceeding to Benghazi and Ankara to Tobruk. At 0100 B/6, the torpedo boat Procione sailed from Piraeus to reinforce the convoy. At 1040 B/6, the two convoys merged and the destroyer Aviere assumed overall command. The combined convoy was now known as convoy 'L'. At 1525 B/6, Procione joined convoy and was thus present during air attack when Manara was torpedoed at 1530 B/6. The air attack was carried out by fourteen to sixteen torpedo bombers, Manara was hit in the stern section and taken in tow by Freccia and escorted by Legionario and Procione. They proceeded to Corfu. At 1855 B/6, Legionario left them to rejoin the convoy. At 1003 B/7, Manara was brought to the Bay of Arilla (near Cape Cavobianco, Corfu). At 1810 B/6, the convoy split. Ankara escorted by the destroyers Fuciliere (which resumed duty as escort leader), Geniere, Lampo, Bombardiere and the torpedo boat Partenope slowed down at 10 knots to distance itself from Ravello and Sestriere escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Legionario, Corsaro, Camicia Nera and the torpedo boats Pallade and Pegaso, which forged ahead. The Ankara section was now known as Convoy 'P', the Ravello section as Convoy 'N'. At 0449 B/7, Legionario rejoined the Ankara convoy. At 0547 B/7, the Ankara convoy sighted the Ravello/Sestriere convoy and at 0737 B/7, the two convoys merged again and were now known, once again as convoy 'L', with Aviere once more assuming overall command. At 0927 B/7, Lampo reported torpedo tracks in position 36°10'N, 21°05'E (this was the attack by HMS P 34) and Sestriere signalled she had avoided torpedoes from the port side by turning to starboard. Lampo was ordered to hunt the submarine. At 1355 B/7, she signalled that the A/S search had not yielded result and that she expected to rejoin the convoy at at about 1900 B/7. She caught up at 1738 B/7. At 1810 B/7, three enemy aircraft attacked the combined convoy which opened up with antiaircraft fire. Bombs fell near Ankara. At 1941 B/7, the convoy split again, Fuciliere assuming command of convoy 'P' which proceeded to Tobruk. Lampo had been assigned to join the escort of the Benghazi convoy, but as it had been reduced to two ships, Fuciliere requested from Aviere that she reinforce the Ankara convoy and this was granted. Convoy 'N' was now formed of Ravello and Sestriere escorted by Aviere, Corsaro, Legionario, Camicia Nera, Pegaso and Pallade. At 0216 B/8, in position 33°30'N, 22°43'E, a low-flying aircraft dropped two or three bombs which narrowly missed Fuciliere (2 metres!) causing massive damage, the shock disabled her gyro compass and her radio but she managed to make 12 knots and remained with the convoy. At 0302 B/8, Fuciliere, by visual signal, ordered Geniere to take over command of the convoy. At 0353 B/8, Fuciliere left the convoy, to make for Crete. Partenope had also turned north to escort her but Fuciliere ordered her to rejoin the convoy. At 0850 B/8, Bombardiere informed Fuciliere that she had been ordered by Geniere to assist her. Fuciliere replied that she was in urgent need of a towing. Bombardiere was proceeding at 25 knots but had trouble locating Fuciliere as the latter could only give a very approximate position due to the breakdown of her gyrocompass. She finally found her at 1830 B/8 near Sphakia and at 2355 B/8 had taken the stricken vessel in tow. At 0729 B/9, Geniere rejoined them. At 0849 B/9, they dropped anchor at Castel Selino (SE Crete) and at 1230 B/9, the Italian destroyer Nicolò Zeno arrived to assist them. At 1810 B/9, the four destroyers left for Navarino where they arrived at 0905 B/10-0919 B/10. In the meantime, at 0636 B/8, the Ankara convoy was joined by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Brioni (1987 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the torpedo boat Orione which had sailed from Suda at 1330 B/7. Ankara, Brioni, Geniere, Lampo, Partenope and Orione arrived at Tobruk at 1315-1428 B/8. Geniere sailed at 1701 B/8 for Taranto but was later diverted to assist Fuciliere (see above). Convoy 'N' arrived at Benghazi at 1021 B/8-1321 B/8. Ravallo, Sestriere, Legionario and Pallade entered harbour while Aviere, Corsaro and Camicia Nera remained outside, awaiting instructions from Supermarina, while Pegaso was detached to assist Fuciliere but eventualle Pegaso was recalled and entered Benghazi at 2000 B/8. (26) 6 Sep 1942 At 0005 B/6, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) set course to the south-west to comply with Capt. S.1's signal timed 2001 C/5. At 0507 B/6, HMS Una dived in position 35°02'N, 22°24'E. At 1945 B/6, HMS Una surfaced in position 34°44'N, 21°58'E. At 2045 B/6, HMS Una received Capt. S.1's signal timed 1850 C/6. Course was set to the northward to comply. (2) 7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 0956 C/7, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN), HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). On completion of attacks submarines should if possible follow up convoys to finish off damaged ships. This should be a standard practice, A/S measures permitting. In a follow up signal timed 1015 C/7, these submarines were informed that Allied aircraft had been ordered not to attack submarines unless positively identified as enemy in areas 'K', 'B' and 'T'. In a signal timed 1514 B/7 and amended in a signal timed 1619 B/7, these submarines were informed by Capt. S.10 that three transports and nine destroyers had been sighted at 1450 B/7 in position 35°30'N, 21°40'N, on course 170° at 10 knots. In a signal timed 2001 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed these submarines that aircraft from Malta hope to attack the enemy convoy about midnight tonight. (26) 7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1837 C/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). If nothing has been sighted by dark proceed patrol area 'K' in sectors from position (A) 35°42'N, 23°28'E as follows: HMS Una, 170° to 215°. HMS P 43, 215° to 235°. P 34, 235° to 270°. Except when in pursuit of enemy submarines should keep to their sector, P 34 and Una not to approach within 5 miles of position (A) and P 43 within 10 miles of position (A). Attention drawn to my signal timed 1015/7th [see above]. (26) 7 Sep 1942 At 0100 B/7, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was in position 35°05'N, 21°51'E. At 0220 B/7, HMS Una received Capt. S.1's signal timed 2115 C/6 was received. Course was set to 250° to comply with the orders. At 0520 B/7, HMS Una dived in position 34°58'N, 21°30'E. From 0900 B/7, distant underwater explosions were heard at frequent intervals. These became louder at 1500 B/7. From 1500 B/7 to 1630 B/7 a total of 16 aircraft were sighted, mostly German JU-88's. They appeared to be carrying out an A/S patrol at heights of about 500-1000 feet. It was considered that HMS Una was on the path of the expected enemy convoy. At 1714 B/7, the mast and funnel of a Grecale-class destroyer was sighted bearing 270° at a range of 7000 yards. Enemy course was 160°. Una turned to the south-west to close. This was the Italian destroyer Lampo which had been ordered to hunt the submarine which had attacked the convoy (HMS P 34). At 1355 B/7, she signalled that the A/S search had not yielded result and that she expected to rejoin the convoy at at about 1900 B/7. She actually caught up at 1738 B/7. At 1740 B/7, the enemy destroyer disappeared from sight. Range had never been below 5000 yards and it was still considered that this destroyer was part of the escort of the expected convoy. However from air reconnaissance reports received afterwards she was well to the westward of it presumably steaming at speed to catch up with the convoy. At 1945 B/7, HMS Una surfaced in position 34°45'N, 21°32'E and set course towards Crete. At 2000 B/7, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1837 C/7 was received. At 2140 B/7, the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1514 B/7 and 1619 B/7 were received. From the signal timed 1619 B/7 it was found that the convoy mist have passed about 15 miles to the eastward of HMS Una around 1630 B/7, although at that time aerial activity pointed to it passing to the westward. At 2142 B/7, received a signal with Time Of Origin (T.O.O.) 1432 B/7 from an aircraft reporting the single destroyer sighted by HMS Una at 1714 B/7. [one destroyer in position 36°05'N. 21°29'E, course 170°, speed 25 knots. A/C call sign FS8D]. (2) 8 Sep 1942 At 0530 B/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) dived in position 35°10'N, 23°00'E. At 2000 B/8, HMS Una surfaced in position 205° - Pondiko Nisi - 9 miles. Remained in the vicinity all night. (2) 9 Sep 1942 At 0440 B/9, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) dived in position 215° - Pondiko Nisi - 6 miles. Maintained patrol to the southwest of the island hoping to intercept the ships mentioned in Capt. S.1's signal timed 1942 C/8 which stated the following; Aircraft reconnaissance reported at 1905 C/8, one large merchant ship and four destroyers in position 33°16'N, 21°17'E, course 039°, speed 13 knots. This must have referred to the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera and Corsaro on passage from Benghazi (departed 1030 B-1120 B/8) to Piraeus (arrived 1643 B/9-1724 B/9). Corsaro left the convoy at 1730 B/9 off Cerigo and and arrived at Navarino at 1300 B/9. At 1005 B/9, smoke was sighted just to the northward of Pondiko Nisi. In a signal timed 1007 C/9, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Una that a submarine had been sighted in her patrol area the previous afternoon. At 1115 B/9, the funnels and bridge of a Navigatori-class destroyer were sighted proceeding on course 190° passing between the Pondiko Nisi and Gramvoussa. Range was 8000 to 9000 yards. On sighted the enemy, HMS Una was in position 255 - Pondiko Nisi - 3.5 miles. This was the destroyer Nicolò Zeno, which was proceeding to Castel Selino to assist the damaged Fuciliere. Intially, Zeno had sailed from Suda for Brindisi at 0057 B/9 but at 0536 B/9 had received an order from Supermarina to return to Suda. She had not reached her destination, when, at 0806 B/9, a new order from Supermarina to proceed to Cape Krio and at 1250 B/8 she was moored next to Fuciliere just east of Castel Selino. At 1120 B/9, the destroyer's speed was estimated as being 18 to 20 knots. In a signal timed 1115 C/9, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Una that at 1005 C/9 aircraft reported a destroyer apparently towing another destroyer in position 35°28'N, 22°45'E, course 324°, speed unknown. This must refer to the Bersagliere towing Fuciliere, however the time does not make sense. There must be an error somewhere. At 2026 B/9, a darkened ship which appeared to be a submarine bearing 090° steering north. Prepared to attack but one minute later the ship was identified as a destroyer. Range was only 1500 yards. At 2030 B/9, the destroyer turned away. Una dived. Between 2030 B/9 and 2100 B/9, HE was heard on all bearings. Course was altered to the south-west. At 2130 B/9, HE faded. At 2230 B/9, Una surfaced. The destroyer appeared to be doing a routine A/S sweep. Una was not detected either on the surface or submerged. At 2330 B/9, Capt. S.1's signal timed 1607 C/9 was received with stated the following; At 1415 C/9 aircraft reported two destroyers anchored in approximate position 35°12'N, 23°32'E. They may be the two destroyers mentioned in my signal timed 1115 C/9. (27) 10 Sep 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) fired torpedoes at the Italian armed merchant cruiser Brioni (1987 GRT, built 1931) about 10 nautical miles west of Paleochora, Crete, Greece in position 35°12'N, 23°29'E. All torpedoes fired missed their target despite the British claim to have sunk the vessel. They sailed from Tobruk at 1804 B/9 and arrived at Piraeus at 0345 B/11. Brioni was en-route from Tobruk (departed 1804 B/9) to Piraeus (arrived 0345 B/11). She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope. Two torpedo tracks were observed by an escorting aircraft but Brioni and Calliope did not see anything and we must assume the torpedoes were quite wide of the mark. 1055 B/10 - Sighed a 4000 tons motor vessel escorted by a destroyer and an aircraft. Enemy course was 340°, speed 8 knots, range approximately 3000 yards. The target was difficult to see due to the heavy sea and spray. Started attack. 1106 B/10 - In position 35°12'N, 23°29'E fired four torpedoes from 1600 yards. 1107 B/10 - Heard one explosion thought to be a hit on the target. HE of the target ceased. The destroyer was heard starting and stopping for the next 30 minutes but did not drop any depth charges. 1145 B/10 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- At 2210 B/10, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2020 C/10 was received which stated the following; If nothing has been sighted by 0200 C/11 leave patrol and return to Malta through position 35°40'N, 19°02'E thence as ordered by Capt. S.10. (2) 11 Sep 1942 At 2355 B/10, west of Crete, in position 35°20'N, 22°50'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 220° at a range of 3000 yards coming towards. HMS Una dived. At 0001 B/11, the destroyer was transmitting on Asdic. Speed of the destroyer, as counted by revs. was about 14 knots. Evasive action was taken. At 0007 B/11, the destroyer passed directly overhead but no depth charges were dropped. The enemy was still transmitting and then started to circle in the vicinity. At 0025 B/11, HE of the destroyer faded. It was considered that HMS Una had been sighted due to the heavy phosphorescent sea and foam breaking over the submarine. The destroyer did not appear to be in contact on Asdic at any time. This was possibly either the destroyer Lampo or torpedo boat Pallade escorting the transport Petrarca (Italian, 3329 GRT, built 1910). They had sailed from Benghazi at 1730 B/9-1835 B/9 and arrived at Piraeus at 2305 B/11-2345 B/11. At 0125 B/11, HMS Una surfaced and continued her passage westwards. In a signal timed 1032 B/11, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una to proceed through positions 35°15'N, 15°01'E and 35°22'N, 14°12'E to arrive at the end of the searched channel at 0959 B/15. If unable to comply make E.T.A. when west of 18°30'E. (27) 14 Sep 1942 Around 1200 B/14, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1010 B/14. (2) 25 Sep 1942 Around 1500 B/25, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol east of Tunisia. She, and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), were escorted out until around 1700 B/25 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). (2) 25 Sep 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during her 13th war patrol see the map below. 29 Sep 1942 At 0205 B/29, in position 100° - Kutiat - 7 miles, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sighted a darkened ship bearing 290°. Closed to investigate. There was a bright moon. At 0230 B/29, the darkened ship was identified as a wreck to the north-west of Kuriat. (2) 30 Sep 1942 At 1950 B/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced in position 35°48'N, 11°09'E, sighted two illuminated ships bearing 330° at a range of 4000 yards. They were on a northerly course. Commenced to chase them at full speed. At 2020 B/30, both ships altered course to port to pass round Kuriat. Range was now about 2000 yards. At 2030 B/30, both ships were proceeding on course 270° passing three miles to the north of Kuriat at a speed of 10 knots. Una was now 1500 yards on their starboard quarter and overtaking. The ships were identified as being a 6000 to 7000 tons merchant vessel in ballast with the French flag floodlit on the ships side. The other vessel was a fully illuminated escort vessel, which was also French. At 2050 B/30, HMS Una was now in line abreast with the escort and merchant ship. Range between the two ships was 1500 yards. At 2055 B/30, the approach was broken off as the French ships were inside territorial waters and obviously making for Sousse or continuing up the Tunisian coast inside territorial waters. Also they were acting in accordance with all regulations. At 2100 B/30, HMS Una proceeded to return to patrol to the south-east of Kuriat. (2) 2 Oct 1942 At 1945 B/2, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced in position 125° - Kuriat - 8 miles. Course was set to the eastward to patrol to the south of Lampione Island the following day. HMS Una was to return to Malta arriving at the end of the swept channel around 1100 B/4, proceeding through position 35°23'N, 13°13'E on the way back. (28) 3 Oct 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) twice fired a torpedoe at the small Italian merchant vessel Doris Ursino (890 GRT, built 1886) inside Lampedusa harbour. The first torpedo fired apparently had defects. Four Italian MAS boats came out to hunt the submarine. This were MAS 533, MAS 557, MAS 560 and MAS 564). MAS 557 and MAS 564 dropped 12 depth charges and claimed to have sunk the submarine (which was incorrect) seven miles south of Lampedusa. [Note that a few Italian documents refer to MAS 537 instead of MAS 557 but they are in error.] 1700 B/3 - Una was now four nautical miles south-west of Lampedusa harbour. Masts and funnels were sighted inside the harbour. Closed to investigate. 1720 B/3 - The harbour was seen to contain the following: - 1 800 to 1000 tons motor vessel, - 2 small tugs, - 1 probable Motor torpedo boat, - Several schooners. 1745 B/3 - Fired one torpedo at the merchant vessel from 2200 yards. No explosion followed. It appeared the torpedo had failed to run. 1749 B/3 - Fired another torpedo from 1800 yards. This torpedo was heard to run. 1750 B/3 - 500 yards to the right of the harbour entrance the torpedo hit the rocks. This torpedo had failed to run straight. Una now retired to the South-East. 1800 B/3 - Small calibre shells from shore batteries were heard to hit the water. 1820 B/3 - High speed HE was heard astern. Shut off for a depth charge attack. 1845 B/3 - One depth charge was heard to explode. 1910 B/3 - Two depth charges were heard to explode. 1940 B/3 - All HE faded. 2027 B/3 - Surfaced in position 120° - Lampedusa Harbour - 7 miles. Proceeded to the eastwards to return to Malta at 10 knots. (2) 4 Oct 1942 Around 1315 B/4, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) ended their 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) and 7th war patrol (also 7th in the Mediterranean) respectively at Malta. They were escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1040 B/2. (29) 12 Oct 1942 Around 1400 A/12, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol east of Sicily / South of Calabria and to carry out a special operation. She to pass through position 35°22'N, 15°12'E about 0259 A/13 for position 37°13'N, 15°50'E. She was escorted out until around 1620 A/12, by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). [ADM 199/424, War Diaries Malta 1942 gives the escort as HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).] (30) 12 Oct 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during her 14th war patrol see the map below. 14 Oct 1942 At 0915 A/14, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) grazed a fishing net from a fishing vessel five miles from the coast. Una went to 80 feet in order not to compromise her position. At 1315 A/14, HMS Una started to close the land once more. At 1735 A/14, in position 070° - Cape Mulini - 9 miles. Set course 282° for the landing position for operation 'Wash Leather' off Stazzo. At 2100 A/14, HMS Una surfaced in position 090° - Stazzo - 2.5 miles. The moon had set. It was a calm moderately dark night. Ideal conditions for a landing. Numerous lights were sighted throughout the towns and villages of the district. Very poor black-out. At 2114 A/14, after having fixed the position of the submarine accurately, opened the fore hatch, placed the Folbot and landing party on the casing. Closed the coast at slow speed. At 2128 A/14, the Folbot and party was disembarked. The Folbot was last seen on the correct course making good headway. Position of disembarkation was 37°38'2"N, 15°13'E, approximately 2000 yards 100° from the landing position. At 2140 A/14, HMS Una commenced to retired to the seaward at slow speed. The water was phosphorescent. The two persens landed were; Agent Emilio Zappalà was born in Catania (Sicily) in 1906 and had been recruited by the French 2e Bureau in Djibouti and then joined the SIS. Radio operator Antonio Gallo born in Sant'Elena,Veneto in 1911 had been borrowed from SOE. Both men were arrested almost immediately and executed on 28 November 1942 at Forte Bravetta. (31) 17 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0526 A/17, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) to keep north-east of position 38°24'N, 16°38'E until 1231 A/18th. The signal only received by HMS Una at 2010 A/17 when she was in position 38°00'N, 16°12'E, and course was set to the north-west to comply. (30) 17 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1715 A/17, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) that a large southbound tanker and two destroyers were reported north of Messina. Enemy speed was 9 to 10 knots. (32) 18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1834 A/18, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) that at 1410 A/18 aircraft had reported three merchant ships and one tug proceeding southwards slowly. On sighting they were 2 miles to the south of of Messina. (32) 18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1029 A/18, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) that at 0901 A/18, aircraft had reported a tanker and two destroyers in position 180° - Cape Spartevento - 1 mile. Enemy course 037° at 8 knots. (32) 18 Oct 1942 While trying to attack the Italian tanker Panuco (7750 GRT, built 1941) off Cape Spartivento, Italy, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is detected and depth charged by the escort. The attack had to be abandoned but HMS Una sustained no damage from the depth charges. At 0325 B/17, the Panuco escorted by the Italian destroyers Maestrale and Grecale sailed from Naples for Tobruk. They stopped at Messina at 0230 B and sailed again at 0545 B/18, reinforced by the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Giuseppe Sirtori but without Maestrale which had to refuel and carry out some repairs. Maestrale finally sailed from Messina at 0605 B/18, and caught up with the convoy off Reggio Calabria. At 1516 B/18, Grecale signalled she had a good sonar contact in position 38°26'N, 18°41'E [handwritten note in text that the position appears to an error as too far from Messina, position should read 38°26'N, 16°41'E]. The destroyer dropped three depth charges and observed a little oil patch and followed by another twelve depth charges. She sighted a torpedo which missed her stern by 40 metres (HMS Una did not fire any torpedo though) and dropped six more depth charges. At 1636 B/18, she had rejoined the convoy. At 1900 B/18, the two torpedo boats were detached and ordered to search for the submarine. At 2237 B/18, Panuco was crippled by a torpedo from an enemy bomber in position 38°28'N, 18°02'E and abandoned. Grecale picked up 101 survivors (including two slightly wounded), one of the crew of the tanker was missing. At 2315 B/18, two bombs were dropped near her but missed by a few hundred metres. The tanker was again missed by a few bombs at 0039 B/19. At 0708 B/19, as the tanker was still afloat, 19 crew members re-embarked and she managed to make 8 knots. Apparently, Giuseppe Sirtori and Giuseppe Dezza were ordered to turn back and rejoin the convoy but the Maestrale report does not mention that they did so. The three ships reached Taranto at 0137 B/20-0300 B/20 and the torpedo boats at 0300 B/20-0415 B/20. 1250 A/18 - Near position 38°26'N, 16°41'E sighted smoke bearing 220°. Started attack. 1330 A/18 - Identified the convoy as a tanker of 10000 tons escorted by three destroyers. Enemy course was 040°. Una was in an excellent position to attack. 1356 A/18 - About five minutes before firing Una was detected by the enemy and forced to go deep and break off the attack. She was hunted by the destroyers. 1433 A/18 - A destroyer astern was in contact and came in to attack. 1435 A/18 - The destroyer went overhead and dropped a pattern of three depth charges very close. No damage was caused. 1440 A/18 - All three destroyers were still hunting. Abandoned the attack and withdrew to the south-east. 1520 A/18 - By now the enemy had lost contact. Two of the destroyers left to rejoin the tanker. The third remained in the vicinity for another 45 minutes before making off to the east. 1700 A/18 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. (31) 19 Oct 1942 At 0350 A/19, in position 37°54'N, 16°33'E, well to the east of Cape Spartivento, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 310° at a range of 4000 yards. Enemy course was 040°. HMS Una dived to avoid being sighted. At 0530 A/19, destroyer HE was picked up on bearing 310°. Revs were counted and the speed was estimated as being 14 knots. The HE faded 20 minutes later. Perhaps there is an error in the course as the 040° is not very clear in the patrol report. A possibility is the torpedo boat Pallade, escorting the Italian transport Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922) and the German transport Menes (5610 GRT, 1926), which had sailed from Taranto at 0530 B/18 and arrived at Messina at 1527 B/19. Otherwise, no other destroyer or torpedo boat should have been in the vicinity. At 2359 A/19, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1115 A/19 was received which started the following; Leave patrol as requisite through position 35°21'N, 15°09'E to make rendezvous with the minesweeper at the end of the swept channel at 1016 A/23. (31) 20 Oct 1942 At 0100 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2339 A/19 which ordered her to patrol vicinity off Cape Colonne until necessary to comply with his signal timed 1115 A/19. As HMS Una was over 120 miles from this position and would never arrive there in time to comply with the Capt. S.10's singal timed 1115 A/19 Lt. Norman decided to compromise and patrol on the Cape Stilo - Cape Spartivento route. Course was set to the north-east accordingly. (33) 21 Oct 1942 At 0430 A/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) left patrol to return to Malta to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1115 A/19. (31) 23 Oct 1942 At 1215 A/23, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0935 A/23. (31) 3 Nov 1942 Around 1415 A/3, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina. She, and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) were swept out until around 1620 A/3 by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). She was to proceed through position 35°22'N, 15°10'E and 36°00'N, 15°??'E [corrupt group in the signal concering the outward route but probably it was 36°00'N, 15°35'E which was Una's position on diving at 0520 A/4.]. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (2) 5 Nov 1942 At 0705 A/5, the the south-west of Cape dell'Armi, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted an A/S trawler starting and stopping. Range was 4 miles. At 0720 A/5, the trawler was heard to be transmitting on Asdic. Range was still 4 miles. At 0730 A/5, HE of the trawler faded. At 0930 A/5, a patrol vessel was sighted bearing 045° at a range of 4 miles. The patrol vessel was proceeding away from HMS Una. At 1200 A/5, masts and funnels of a northbound two-funnelled hospital ship were sighted bearing 045°. Position was 230° - Cape dell'Armi - 9 miles. This was most probably the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, 1928) which sailed from Tobruk at 1655 A/3 and arrived at Naples at 0712 A/6. (2) 6 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1916 A/6, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) that air reconnaissance had shown an enemy submarine position 36°20'N, 18°00'E at 1752 A/6 on course 310° at 10 knots. Utmost was ordered to patrol in position 37°53'N, 15°44'E by 0700 A/7. This may have referred to the Italian submarine Ascianghi which was on passage to Augusta from a supply mission to Tobruk. (34) 7 Nov 1942 At 0800 A/7, to the south-west of Cape dell'Armi, in position 37°54'N, 15°37'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sighted a Messina bound coastal convoy of three small merchant vessels escorted by a torpedo boat. These were not attacked in accordance with the patrol orders. This was a convoy made up of the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori which had sailed from Taranto at 2250 A/5 for Crotone from where she escorted Orsolina Bottiglieri (Italian, 883 GRT, built 1899), Sacro Cuore (Italian, 1097 GRT, built 1899) and Nerucci (Italian, 1235 GRT, built 1892), sailing at 0700 A/6 for Messina where they arrived at 1250 A/7. (35) 7 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1943 A/7, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) that air reconnaissance had shown an enemy submarine position 36°12'N, 18°14'E at 1706 A/7 on course 310° at 11 knots. This may have referred to the Italian submarine Granito which was on passage to Augusta from a supply mission to Tobruk. (34) 8 Nov 1942 At 1300 A/7, to the south-west of Cape dell'Armi, in position 37°50'N, 15°34'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sighted an A/S schooner bearing 140° at a range of 3 miles. The enemy was proceeding to the north. At 1315 A/7, the schooner stopped. Range was now 3000 yards. At 1320 A/7, the schooner got underway again. She was about 150 to 200 tons, two-masted with an auxiliary diesel engine. A light gun was seen forward. She was painted dark grey and was flying the Italian Ensign. (2) 9 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 2307 A/9, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) of the following; At 1750 A/9 air reconnaissance shows three cruisers and six destroyers in position 36°56'N, 21°11'E, course 273°, speed 23 knots. Move with all despatch to the following positions; Una to 080° - Cape Santa Croce - 5 miles and Utmost 100° - Cape Santa Croce - 1/4 mile. [Cape Santa Crose is off Augusta.] In a signal timed 0025 A/10, these positions were amended to the following positions; Una 112° - Cape Santa Croce - 7 miles and Utmost 117° - Cape Santa Croce - 13 miles. (34) 10 Nov 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) fired four torpedoes at an enemy force of three light cruisers escorted by six destroyers. No hits were obtained. The cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi (8th Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Granatiere and Camicia Nera sailed from Navarino at 1630 A/9-1835 A/9 at 25 knots and arrived at Augusta at 0700 A/10-0830 A/10. Later the same day Una attacked but missed an Italian submarine with one torpedo. This was the Wolframio (T.V. Giovanni Manunta) who reported the torpedo missing her by 50 metres and fired three rounds with her deck gun in the direction of Una. 0610 A/10 - In position 37°11'N, 15°30'E sighted the enemy cruiser force Una was informed about by signal. They were sighted at bearing 100° at a distance of 5 nautical miles. Enemy speed was very high, 25 knots. The cruiser force consisted of three light cruisers escorted by six destroyers, three one each wing. Started attack. 0618 A/10 - The rear ship of the line was now chosen as targe as Una had not been able to turn quickly enough to attack the two first cruisers. 0625 A/10 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes at a range of 4000 yards. 0633 A/10 - Heard two torpedo explosions which might have been hits a 8000 to 10000 yards. Una meanwhile withdrew at 80 feet to the east-north-east. ----------------------------------------- 1202 A/10 - Heard HE bearing 110°. 1203 A/10 - Sighted the conning tower of a submarine. Torpedo reload had not been carried out so now one tube was reloaded in 7.5 minutes. 1221 A/10 - In position 37°25'N, 15°35'E fired one torpedo that at first ran straight for the enemy but then diverged slightly passing up her starboard side. It appears the attack was not noticed by the enemy [this was not the case though]. (2) 10 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0025 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN); My signal timed 2307 A/9. Amend submarine positions to read as follows. HMS Una 112° - Cape Santa Croce - 7 miles and HMS Utmost 117° - Cape Santa Croce - 13 miles. In a signal timed 1154 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN); My signal timed 2307 A/9. The three cruisers are now in Augusta. Return to patrol positions given in the operation order of Operation Torch (Submarines) at economical speed. (34) 11 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1010 A/11, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) that three cruisers and four destroyers were approaching her position. No further details were given in this signal. Nothing of this force was sighted though. The cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi (8th Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Granatiere and Camicia Nera sailed from Augusta at 0520 A/11-0626 A/11 and arrived at Messina at 1032 A/11-1300 A/11. At 2045 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1754 A/11 ordering her to return to Malta via reverse route of her outward route. She was to arrive at the end of the swept channel off Malta at 1201 A/13. (36) 13 Nov 1942 Around 1415 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean), and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (also 8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. At 1100 A/13, HMS Una arrived off the end of the searched channel and made contact with HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). At 1250 A/13, HMS P 44 also joined and the submarines proceeded towards the harbour escorted by HMS Hythe. (37) 20 Nov 1942 Around 1120 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Tunis. She was escorted out by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). (2) 20 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. 20 Nov 1942 At 2330 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a submarine 5 miles on the port beam which was thought to be either HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) or HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN). Avoiding action was taken. At 2334 A/20, south-south-west of Licata, Sicily, in position 36°37'N, 13°36'E, HMS P 35 sighted HMS Una on an opposite course. Avoiding action was taken to the westward. (38) 21 Nov 1942 At 1650 A/21, south-east of Marsala, Sicily, in approximate position 37°36'N, 12°18'E, during the passage through Q.B.B. 255, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted two trawlers and two seaplanes patrolling about 10 miles to the south of Marettimo Island. (2) 21 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1541 A/21 Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) of the following; With air and light surface night patrols and moonlight condition it will be prudent to withdraw to seaward for charging in order to maintain offensive. Recommend following route. Una from position 37°10, 10°35 course 012°. Utmost from Cape Guardia 360° or from Canirock 060°. (39) 21 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 2320 A/21, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN) of the following; Convoy report consider correct interpretation, five destroyers, six merchant ships course 165° speed 4 [probably a mistake in the signal 10 is more likely] knots. Probably split and enter both ports [Bizerta and Tunis]. Objects to attack are the merchant ships. Aircraft will drop flares hourly. HMS Una intercept south of line Plane Island - Zembra Island, HMS P 247 north of this line. This signal must have referred to a convoy mentioned in the following air reconnaissance reports; Position of ten unknown units 38°45'N, 09°20'E. (A/C Call Sign W5U duty 'U', 2110/21 to A.H.Q. Malta) One cruiser, four destroyers, six transports in position 38°37'N, 09°25'E, course 180° at 10 knots. (A/C W5UU, 2200 A/21 to A.H.Q. Malta.) Aircraft are to attack immediately, A.H.Q. Malta's signal timed 2215 A/21 to aircraft; W5U duty 'X', W5U duty 'C' and W5U duty 'S'. Position of the enemy, 38°32'N, 09°32'E, course 180°, speed 10 knots. (A/C W5UX, 2235 A/21 to A.H.Q. Malta.) One cruiser, four destroyers, four motor torpedo boats and five transports in position 38°15'N, 09°25'E. (A/C W5UU, 2349 A/21 to to A.H.Q. Malta.) There is no convoy in this area which fits the discription of the air reconnaissance report. The only southbound convoy in this area at that period is the one made up of the transport Caterina Costa (Italian, 8060 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Vincenzo Gioberti and Corsaro. They had sailed from Naples at 0620 A/21 and arrived at Bizerte at 0750 A/22. The two destroyers sailed from Bizerte at 0820 A/22 and arrived at Cagliari at 1623 A/22 to pick up elements of the San Marco Regiment (Italian Marines) and sailed again at 0100 A/23, arriving at Bizerte at 0830 A/24. All available destroyers and torpedo boat files have been checked. There was no deployment of five destroyers in this area at that time, not even three! We have not been able to find any evidence of such a convoy at all. The schooner Triglia left Trapani at 0900 A/21 via Porto Empedocle and arriveed at Tunis at 2000 A/23. The schooner Esmeralda left Trapani at 0145 A/21 and arrived at Tunis at 0830 A/22. (39) 22 Nov 1942 At 0153 A/22, well to the south-west of Marettimo Island, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), sighted a trawler on converging course on the port beam. HMS Una altered course to starboard to avoid. A second trawler was sighted on the starboard bow. At 0510 A/22, about 10 miles to the north of Zembra Island, an A/S schooner was seen patrolling at a very slow speed. At 0542 A/22, near Zembra Island, HMS Una dived to avoid a trawler and an A/S schooner (one on either bow). Dawn was also breaking so remained dived. The A/S schooner remained in sight until 1430 A/22. During the day, HMS Una patrolled in the middle of the Gulf of Tunis. A lot of enemy transport planes were sighted during the day. (2) 22 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0155 A/22, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN) were informed by Capt. S.10 of the following; Speed of convoy 10, mean course to midnight 180°. Consider land fall may be Cape Angela about 0500 hours. Still probably some ships destination Tunis. (39) 23 Nov 1942 At 0613 A/23, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 37°02'N, 10°33'E and spent the day in the centre and south of the Gulf of Tunis. At 1415 A/23, HMS Una sighted a large column of smoke behind Cape Carthage but nothing developed. A large number of formations of enemy transport planes were sighted during the afternoon. At 1829 A/23, HMS Una surfaced in position 37°08'N, 10°31'E and patrolled while charging in the entrance to the Gulf of Tunis. (2) 24 Nov 1942 At 0620 A/24, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 37°01'N, 10°37'E and patrolled during the day in the Gulf of Tunis. At 1720 A/24, Una sighted a small two-masted schooner leaving Tunis. The schooner was starting and stopping engines and possibly sending out A/S transmissions. At 1801 A/24, HMS Una surfaced. The schooner was not in sight. At 2043 A/24, HMS Una had to dive for an aircraft searching with a searchlight. She surfaced at 2136 A/23. (2) 25 Nov 1942 At 0621 A/25, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 37°04'N, 10°41'E and patrolled during the day in the Gulf of Tunis. At 1821 A/25, Una surfaced. Nothing of importance had been sighted during the day. (2) 26 Nov 1942 At 0608 A/26, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 37°03'N, 10°32'E and patrolled during the day in the Gulf of Tunis and inspected La Gouletta roads. The masts of a merchant vessels of about 5000 tons were seen inside the breakwater. At 1820 A/26, HMS Una surfaced. (2) 26 Nov 1942in a signal timed 1809 A/26, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) to patrol in position 063° - Cani rock lighthouse - 31 miles. It was also mentioned that it was imported not to be sighted by enemy aircraft. (39) 27 Nov 1942 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) attacked an enemy convoy with three torpedoes north of the Gulf of Tunis in position 37°34'N, 10°33'E. All torpedoes missed their intended target despite the claim for a hit. This convoy attacked was made up of the Italian transports Honestas (4932 GRT, built 1920), Sant'Antioco (4994 GRT, built 1919) and the German barge FP 477. They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Ciclone and Ardente. They heard two underwater explosions. This convoy had sailed from Naples at 0330 A/25-0430 A/25 (except FP 477 which had sailed from Palermo at 2100 A/25 and joined off Trapani). At 2115 A/26, FP 477 escorted by Ardente were detached to proceed to Bizerte but for unclear reasons, the German lighter refused to obey the order and finally they all arrived at Tunis at 0630 A/27-0805 A/27. Later on the same day HMS Una made another attack on a single escorted transport. Four torpedoes were fired but again no hits were obtained. Apparently there was a second transport as this attack was against the convoy made up of the transports Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940) and Sestriere (7992 GRT, built 1942) escorted by destroyers Maestrale, Grecale, Ascari and Camicia Nera proceeding to Bizerte where they arrived at 1400 A/27-1510 A/27. They had sailed from Naples at 1245 A/26-1500 A/26. 2120 A/26 - Received a report from an aircraft that it had just sighted two transports and two destroyers proceeding towards the position of Una. Set course to the north to intercept. 2200 A/26 - Sighted flares and AA gunfire to the northward. 2233 A/26 - Sighted aircraft flares bearing 020°. 2358 A/26 - Sighted aircraft flares bearing 040°. 0004 A/27 - Sighted two transports and one destroyer bearing 235°. Altered course to close. 0006 A/27 - Dived, the enemy meanwhile changed course to 185°. Stared attack. 0047 A/27 - Fired three torpedoes from 1500 yards. 50 Seconds after firing a very loud explosion was heard which broke numerous glass fittings in Una as well as causing some other minor damage. As this gave a running range of 1200 yards it is thought that this was a torpedo hitting the target. The intended fouth torpedo of the salvo was now not fired. Evasive action was taken but Una was not counter attacked. -------------------------------------- 1128 A/27 - Observed three destroyers circling about three nautical miles to the north. 1132 A/27 - Sighted a transport of 3000 to 4000 tons steering about 240°. Una was right ahead at a range of about 8000 yards. It was assumed that Una was at the place of a rendezvous. Started attack. 1226 A/27 - Near position in position 37°34'N, 10°47'E fired four torpedoes from 1200 yards. Una went deep upon firing. No sounds of a hit were heard. 1236 A/27 - Retured to periscope depth. The target was seen to proceed to the south-west with one of the destroyers. The other two destroyers were proceeding to the north. (2) 27 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 0747 A/27, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) to move 15 miles 310° and HMS Tribune (Lt. N.J. Coe, DSC, RNR) to move 3 miles 310° both at 5 knots submerged. Four transports and seven destroyers had been reported at 0635 A/27 in position 38°40'N, 11°30'E proceeding on course 220° at speed 14 knots. This appears to be a bit too far to the west and we suspect the aircraft made an error in the position reported. Visibility was not very good on that day. This could have referred to the Monginevro/Sestriere convoy [see previous event]. It may also refer to the Città di Napoli convoy which arrived at Bizerte at about the same time as the Monginevro convoy. Perhaps they were fairly close at sea when the aircraft sighted them and my have erroneously concluded that they formed a single convoy. However, from their reports the two convoys do not appear to have sighted each other but again visibility was rather poor. The transports Città di Napoli (Italian, 5418 GRT, built 1930) and Città di Tunisi (Italian, 5419 GRT, built 1930) sailed from Palermo at 2215 A/26-2330 A/26 escorted by the Italian destroyers Mitragliere, Corazziere and Folgore At 2020 A/26, the torpedo boat Climene sailed from Trapani to reinforce the convoy. At 0110 A/27, they sighted convoy 'LL; coming from the opposite direction. These were the transports Zenobia Martini (Italian, 1454 GRT, built 1911) and Giuseppe Leva (Italian, 1430 GRT, built 1907) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Circe. At 0013 A/27, in position 38°14'N, 12°57'E, Circe suddenly changed course and the Città di Tunisi rammed her accidentally, sinking the torpedo boat. Folgore picked up 99 survivors and was ordered by Mitragliere to escort the two merchant vessels of convoy LL to Palermo where they arrived at 1000 A/27. At 0205 A/27, the torpedo boat Climene joined the Città di Napoli convoy. As Città di Tunisi had a leak in her bow, she was detached at 0400 A/27 to Trapani, escorted by Climene and they arrived at 1755 A/27. Città di Napoli, escorted by Mitragliere and Corazziere arrived at Bizerte at 1415 A/27. It is possible that the aircraft sighted the two merchant ships and four destroyers of the Monginevro convoy and the Città di Napoli and her two destroyers. It is doubtful that Città di Tunisi was sighted as she was now slower and certainly not on a 220° course as she was steering for Trapani. (39) 27 Nov 1942 At 1935 A/27, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) passed her signal timed 1401 A/27, to Capt. S.10. This signal stated the following; One torpedo remaining. Intend returning direct to position B. and pass through QBB 255 at daylight on the 28th. In a signal timed 1705 A/27, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines on patrol that HMS Utmost was overdue and a signal from her may have resulted in her loss as she did not appear to have made a diversion from her course after sending the signal and it was feared she may have been caught by enemy patrol vessel. Lt. Martin, decided not to start the passage of QBB 255 that night a message informing Capt. S.10 of this was passed around 0540 A/28. (40) 27 Nov 1942 At 2248 A/27, about mid-way between the Bay of Tunis and Sicily, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a darkened ship and dived to evade. HE was picked up from three different directions. At 0300 A/28, Ten depth charges were heard to explode about 1 to 2 miles away. At 0445 A/28, HMS Una surfaced. During the time she was submerged pairs of ships hunting were heard almost continuously. (2) 28 Nov 1942 At 0547 A/28, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived and during the day she patrolled about 20 miles to the west of Marettimo Island. She surfaced at 1805 A/28 and set course to return to Malta. (2) 29 Nov 1942 At 0838 A/29, while south-south-west of Marsala, Sicily, in approximate position 37°41'N, 12°25'E, while coming to periscope depth to obtain a land fix, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted two trawlers passing about 1.5 miles inshore of her in line abreast. Also a trawler and a schooner, the latter flying a large red flag, in line abreast were seen 2 miles away. HMS Una was right ahead of them. At 0845 A/29, HMS Una touched bottom with 45 feet on the depth gauge. In view of the enemy patrol vessels being quite near it was considered best to remain on the bottom until they had passed. All motors were stopped and 'Q' tank was flooded to keep the submarine down. One of the enemy vessels was pinging. The enemy was heard to pass close by and then seemed to remain astern while searching. By 0943 A/29, HE had faded and 'Q' tank was blown. Una went full ahead and turned 180° and managed to get clear of the bank without breaking surface. Position of the grounding was 37°41'N, 12°27'E. At 1045 A/29, HMS Una again came to periscope depth to ascertain her position and it was seen that Una was now ahead of another pair of trawlers which were proceeding to the north-west. They were flying red flags and two black balls and possibly minesweeping. HMS Una ran out two miles to avoid them. At 1611 A/29, HMS Una communicated on SS/T with HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN) which was also Malta-bound. At 2213 A/29, in approximate position 37°00'N, 12°45'E, two torpedo tracks were seen approaching from the starboard side on a 120° track. The first torpedo would have passed close ahead and the second one hit about 15 feet abaft the stem had not the Officer of the Watch, Lt. T.W. Lancaster, RN) immediately put the helm hard over to port. The second torpedo passed about 10 feet ahrad, so close that it's engine was heard by two ratings in the fore-ends. The prompt action of Lt. Lancaster undoubtedly saved the submarine. The night was dark. About one-third of the horizon was as black as the sea, due to rain squalls, the other two-thirds were lighter than the sea. At 2215 A/29, HMS Una dived. After 40 minutes, the HE of two motor torpedo boats were heard starting up. Apparently they had been waiting for Una to reach Malta on the morning of 30 November and it was decided to stay down until 0230 A/30. (2) 1 Dec 1942 Around 1300 A/1, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta where she was almost immediately docked in no.1 graving dock together with HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) which was docked again after already having been in dock from 27 to 30 November. HMS Una had been escorted in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined at 1025 A/1. (41) 10 Dec 1942 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) were undocked. (42) 12 Dec 1942 Around 1500 A/12, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the east coast of Tunisia between 35°00'N and latitude Kuriat, west of 12°00'E. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (2) 13 Dec 1942 At 1314 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) received a signal from Capt. S.10 [no time of the signal given in the patrol report and we have been unable to find a signal addressed to HMS Una.]. Course was set for Kerkennah no.4 buoy to comply with this signal. (2) 18 Dec 1942 At 2017 A/18, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1745 A/17, which ordered her to be in position off Mahedia by dawn 19th. Course was set accordingly. (43) 20 Dec 1942 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) made a torpedo attack on the small German transport Dora (584 GRT, built 1898) near Kuriat, Tunisia. No hits were obtained. 0913 A/20 - While patrolling south-east of Kuriat sighted a southbound merchant ship of about 2000 tons. She was escorted by two aircraft. Started attack. 0938 A/20 - Near position 35°38'N, 11°13'E fired four torpedoes from 2800 yards. No explosions were heard and the torpedoes must have missed. (2) 21 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2100 A/21, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) to leave patrol after dark on the 22nd and return to Malta through position 35°17'N, 12°35'E to make rendezvous at the end of the Malta swept channel on 24 December. (43) 24 Dec 1942 Around 1015 A/24, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0745 A/24. (2) 4 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/4, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily to the south of 38°43'N and between longitudes 12°45'E and 13°33'E. At the same time HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (also 11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Sicilian Channel to the south of the Skerki Bank (to patrol area west of line Keith Rock - Cape Bon and south of QBB 201). They parted company around 1515 A/15, shortly after clearing the swept channel. The submarines were not escorted out. The submarines were routed through position 35°45'N, 12°34'E and then through positions 'K' (36°24'N, 11°49'E), 'J' (37°04'N, 11°40'E) and 'H' (37°26'N, 11°07'E). From position 'H', HMS Una was to proceed to position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) and then to her patrol area. (37) 4 Jan 1943 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during her 18th war patrol see the map below. 5 Jan 1943 At 2305 A/5, near Pantelleria, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a small darkened ship. HMS Una dived to avoid being sighted surfacing again at 0348 A/6. Between 0110 A/6 and 0145 A/6 fast HE was picked up moving up the starboard side from aft to forward. Between 0200 A/6 and 0210 A/6 HE was heard from the vessels starting and stopping and starting again. At 0215 A/6, HE faded. It was thought to have been an enemy motor torpedo boat. (2) 6 Jan 1943 At 1900 A/6, well to the east-north-east of Cape Bon, Tunisia, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted the white phosphorescent wash of small craft on the starboard beam. She dived and fast HE was heard for about half an hour. HMS Una surfaced at 2015 A/6 and continued her passage. (2) 7 Jan 1943 In signals timed 1731 A/7 and 1839 A/7, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) to establish a patrol line to the north of Ustica. [We have not been able to find the text of these signals]. This was to try to intercept the Italian tanker Saturno (former French Massis, 5022 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Pallade and Sirio which an ULTRA intercept had shown as expected to leave Naples for Bizerte at 0100 A/8 (see DEFE3/7/frame 86) and pass through position 38°28'N, 12°05'E at 2300 A/8, Actually the convoy sailed from Naples at 2330 A/7-0115 A/8, but Pallade had a variety of defects and only caught up with the tanker and Sirio at 1216 A/8. At 1555 A/8, a fire occurred on Saturno and the trio was diverted to Palermo, where they arrived at 1400 A/9. They sailed from Palermo at 1430 A/10-1445 A/10. At 1705 A/10, in position 298° - Cape Gallo – 4.2 miles, Pallade detected an echo at a range of 4000 metres. Saturno and Sirio turned hard to port to avoid it, while Pallade proceeded to attack the submarine which was moving at 3 knots. At 1714 A/10, twelve 180-kg depth charges were dropped, another pattern followed at 1730 A, with a third pattern of twelve depth charges at 1800. It appeared from the sonar that the target had broken in two. Pallade tried to recover debris but in the darkness could not find any. And at 1937 A/10 had rejoined the convoy. In the meantime, the Italian corvettes Antilope, Artemide and Gabbiano (1st Corvette Squadron, equipped with SAFAR P.600 sonar) sailed from Trapani at 1800 A/10-1820 A/10. They proceeded on A/S search ahead of the convoy. At 1000 A/11, a German aircraft dropped several bombs on a submarine and Antilope proceeded to look for it, She obtained a contact and dropped a pattern of depth charges at 1015 A/11 in position 37°26'N, 10°39'E, and another at 1115 A/11, observing a patch of oil. Artemide followed up with a pattern of depth charges at 1208 A/11 and another at 1420 A/11. The three corvettes returned to Trapani, arriving at 2330 A/11-0120 A/12. Saturno and her two torpedo boats arrived at Bizerte at 1450 A/11-1655 A/11. (44) 8 Jan 1943 At 0614 A/8, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived in her patrol position and she dived in 39°16'N, 13°18'E. Nothing was seen all day. Enemy reports showed the convoy passing to the west of the patrol line. HMS Una proceeded westwards to try to intercept but nothing was seen. (2) 8 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1605 A/8, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) to shift their patrol line 20 miles to the northward. In a signal timed 2252 A/8, they were ordered to return to their original patrol areas. [We have been unable to find the exact text of these signals.] (44) 10 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1136 A/10, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) to form a patrol line to the north-west of Ustica by 0001 A/11. [We have not been able to find the text of this signal.] (37) 10 Jan 1943 At 1705 A/10, west of Cape Gallo and east of Cape San Vito, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was detected by the Italian torpedo boat Pallade escorting the Italian tanker Saturno (5029 GRT, built 1914) with the torpedo boat Sirio. Later the Italian corvettes Antilope, Artemide and Gabbiano. Pallade carried out a sweep of the area also carrying out depth charge attacks which were reported by the submarine [between 1706 and 1805 hours] as 'fairly close'. HMS Una but did not sighted the tanker and the other escorts. [See the event for 7 January 1943 for more info on this convoy and the A/S group made up of the corvettes.] (2) 11 Jan 1943 At 0137 A/11, west of Ustica, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) heard fast HE coming from the direction of a black cloud to the eastward. At first this was thought to be rain. At 0212 A/11, the HE rapidly became louder and the noise of what was thought to be a motor torpedo boat's engine was heard. HMS Una dived. The HE remained in the vicinity stopping and starting for about one and a half hours. At 0528 A/11, HMS Una surfaced. It was considered that the vessel had been the starboard wing escort of a convoy which aircraft later reported as passing HMS Una to the eastward at this time. The main body of the convoy probably passed at least 7 miles from HMS Una as only one set of HE was heard. This was also confirmed by the position given by the aircraft report. A large ship would probably not have been visible more than half a mile against the black background. At 0619 A/11, HMS Una dived in position 38°49'N, 12°52'E. She spent the day patrolling to the west of Ustica. Visibility was not good, as it rained all day. It had indeed been a convoy passing through the area. The convoy consisted of the transports Mario Rosselli (Italian, 6835 GRT, built 1942), Manzoni (Italian, 4550 GRT, built 1942) and Alfredo Oriani (Italian, 4308 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian destroyer Camicia Nera and the Italian torpedo boats Clio, Ardito and Ardente. They had sailed from Naples at 1545 A/10-1600 A/10 and arrived at Bizerte at 1805 A/11. The four escort vessels did not enter Bizerte but sailed immediately for Italian ports. Camicia Nera and Clio arrived at Naples at 1700 A/12. Ardente and Ardito were ordered to Palermo, but Ardente collided with the destroyer Grecale at 0403 A/12 in position 242° - Cape San Vito – 6 miles. The destroyer had rammed her between the funnel and the bridge, causing a large fire. Ardente sank at 0500 A/12 in position 008° - Punta Barone – 3 miles. The destroyers Grecale and Nicolò Zeno had sailed from Palermo at 0105 A/12, carrying German troops and their stores for Tunisia. At 1241 A/12-1315 A/12, Zeno and Grecale returned to Palermo. Ardito searched for survivors of Ardente, found 21 of them and arrived at Palermo at 1430 A/13. (2) 11 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1631 A/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN). After dark tonight move to established patrol as follows. HMS Una in the area south of latitude 038°45'N, and between longitudes 14°00'E and 14°50'E. HMS P 44 in the area east of 14°20'E and between latitudes 39°00'N and 40°10'N. Enter your area by the south western corner. You may spend up to 48 hours gunning trains. (45) 12 Jan 1943 At 1003 A/12, north-west of Cape d'Orlando, in approximate position 38°16'N, 14°38'E, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a Confienza-class torpedo boat which passed 2000 yards to the seaward proceeding to the westward. This was probably the Italian destroyer Premuda which sailed at 1400 A/11 from Taranto and arrived at 1455 A/12 at Palermo. From 0955 A/12 to 1120 A/12, she was between Cape Milazzo and Cape Orlando. At 1030 A/12, an 1100 tons merchant vessel, in ballast, was seen hugging the coast eastbound. Also two schooners were seen close inshore. This was most probably the Salona (Italian, 664 GRT, built 1913) which had sailed from Palermo at 1100 A/11 at a speed of 6 knots bound for Messina (no info on her arrival time). At 1630 A/12, a very large tug towing two barges passed 1000 yards inshore of HMS Una. They were westbound. This was possibly the tug Salvatore Primo (716 GRT, built 1902) which sailed from Milazzo at 1200 A/12 and returned at 1635 A/13. [No clue on where she went and the purpose of this sortie.] (2) 14 Jan 1943 At 1600 A/14, north-west of Cape d'Orlando, in approximate position 38°12'N, 14°42'E, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a Sirtori-class torpedo boat passing closing inshore proceeding eastwards at a speed of 20 knots. This was the Giuseppe Dezza which had sailed from Palermo at 1230 A/14 and arrived at Messina at 2100 A/14. (2) 14 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1755 A/14, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN). Leave patrol P.M. Jan. 15th and return Malta. HMS Una through position 180° - Ustica - 10 miles thence direct to point 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) and by usual route to arrive at Malta 1500 A/l8. HMS P 44 through position 360° - Ustica = 10 miles thence direct to position 'G' and by usual route to arrive at Malta 1300 A/l9. Speed to be adjusted to pass 'G', HMS Una at 2000 A/16 and HMS P 44 24 hours later. (45) 15 Jan 1943 At 1600 A/15, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1141 A/15, which ordered HMS Una to be in position 33°30'N, 12°50'E [this is the position mentioned in the patrol report but this is obviously an error as this is off Tripoli, Libya and should be 38°30'N, 12°50'E, which is to the north of Cape San Vito, Sicily] by 0400 A/16. As HMS Una was at the furthest end of her patrol area and the wind was west-north-west force 6, it was impossible to reach this position in time. HMS Una immediately surfaced and proceeded. During the night there was a full westerly gale. Revolutions for 9.5 knots gave only 5.5 knots. At 2015 A/15, HMS Una shipped a big sea down the conning tower hatch which flooded the control room to depth of one foot but there was no damage. Had HMS Una been able to be in the position ordered on time a torpedo attack would not have been possible in these weather conditions. The reason HMS Una was ordered to this position was to intercept a convoy made up of the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) and the Italian transport Emma (7931 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Groppo, Clio and Uragano. They sailed from Naples at 1430 A/15-1530 A/15 [this was known through ULTRA, see DEFE3/858/page 593]. At 1950 A/15, in very bad weather, Emma signalled that her engine room was flooded. At 2002 A/15, Emma was torpedoed by HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN) and badly damaged. Because of of the gale, Groppo and Clio were unable to tow the stricken ship and four tugs were sent from Naples for the task. At 2006 A/15, Groppo signalled Ankara and Uragano to proceed to their destination, but at 2105 A/15, Uragano informed Ankara that she was unable to keep up and that the German ship had to proceed independently, eventually she was diverted to Palermo. Groppo and Clio returned to Naples at 0705 A/16-0816 A/16, Uragano arrived at 1200 A/16. Ankara sailed from Palermo at 2245 A/17-2340 A/17, escorted by the destroyer Saetta and the torpedo boat Uragano. At 1410 A/18, in position 37°23.5'N, 10°13'E, Ankara hit a mine, Uragano picked up 26 Italian and 92 German passengers. The crew remained on board attempting to get the ship underway but it was hopeless and 119 survivors were picked up by Saetta. Ankara sank at 1529 A/18. Saetta landed the survivors at Palermo at 0212 A/19. Uragano arrived at Bizerte at 1725 A/18 where she landed her military survivors. (2) 16 Jan 1943 At 0624 A/16, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 38°31'N, 13°31'E (north of Palermo Bay / south-east of Ustica) which was still well short of the ordered patrol position. (2) 16 Jan 1943 In signals timed 0836 A/16 and 1352 A/16 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 54 (Lt. J. Whitton, RN); If nothing has been sighted by 1200 A/16; HMS P 54 proceed direct to position 38°28'N, 08°01'E thence as ordered by Capt. S.8 to Algiers. HMS Una proceeds by usual route to arrive at the end of the Malta searched channel at 1100 A/19. Passage through QBB 255 should be made at least two hours ahead of HMS P 44. In a signal timed 1721 A/16, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 54 to establish patrol 11 miles north-west of Marettimo lighthouse by 0100 A/17th. In a signal timed 1725 A/16, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una to patrol to the north-west of Cape St. Vito by 2200 A/16. In a signal timed 2355 A/16, to HMS Una and HMS P 54 the Capt. S.10 ordered them to proceed according to his signal 0856 A/16 if nothing had been sighted by 0100 A/17 for HMS Una and 0400 A/17 for HMS P 54. The submarines had been positioned (due to ULTRA information) to intercept a convoy made up of the German transport Ruhr (5954 GRT, built 1925) and the Italian transport Col di Lana (5891 GRT, built 1926), escorted by the German motor minesweeper R 14 and the Italian torpedo boats Animoso, Ardito and Generale Antonino Cascino which were expected through ULTRA to leave Palermo at 1800 A/16, speed 10 knots, and to pass point T 3 (off Trapani) at 0230 A/17 (see DEFE3/858/page 530). The convoy actually sailed from Palermo at 1615 A/17-1727 A/17, and was really made up of the above mentioned Col di Lana and the German transports Gerda Toft (1960 GRT, built 1930) and Henri Estier (former French, 1984 GRT, built 1930) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Animoso, Ardito and Generale Antonino Cascino and the German motor minesweepers R 14 and R 16. At 1340 A/18, they sighted the Ankara convoy (this was shortly before the German ship hit a mine, see the event for 15 January 1943 (on the page of HMS Una) for more info on the passage of the Ankara). At 1956 A/18-2020 A/18, the convoy arrived at Bizerte. (45) 17 Jan 1943 At 0005 A/17, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived in the ordered patrol position as in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1725 A/17. She was late in arriving as this signal had also been received rather late. At 0117 A/17, faint HE was heard bearing east. This were thought to be three destroyers reported by aircraft as passing close inshore round Cape St. Vito. Nothing was seen though. These were the Italian destroyers Mitragliere, Premuda and Nicolò Zeno, carrying German and Italian troops, which had sailed from Palermo at 2235 A/16-2255 A/16 and arrived at Tunis at 1005 A/17-1010 A/17. At 2025 A/17, north of Marettimo, in approximate position 38°09'N, 12°05'E, a small darkened ship was sighted steering westwards from the direction of Trapani. At 2041 A/17, HMS Una dived as this ship was seen to rapidly approaching HMS Una. At 2059 A/17, the ship was seen to be a Spica-class torpedo boat which passed close astern of HMS Una at 17 knots. Before diving a column of smoke had been seen. Whilst dived a trawler was seen to pass Marettimo on course 250° at about 7 knots. The torpedo boats was probably either Animoso, Ardito or Generale Antonino Cascino (the last one unlikely given her different appearance) which were escorting the transports Col di Lana (Italian, 5891 GRT, built 1926), Gerda Toft (German 1960 GRT, built 1935) and Henri Estier (German, former 0French, 1984 GRT, built 1930). They had sailed from Palermo at 1615 A/17-1727 A/17 and arrived at Bizerte at 1956 A/18-2020 A/18. At 2234 A/17, HMS Una surfaced and proceeded. At 2345 A/17, two small dark shapes were sighted on the starboard bow. Course was altered to evade and the objects disappeared from sight after about 10 minutes. (2) 18 Jan 1943 At 0045 A/18, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was abeam of the trawler that had first been seen the previous evening. It was now seen that there were three small dark shapes proceeding about half a mile ahead of her. These were either self propelled barges, motor torpedo boats or a mix of both. At 0047 A/18, aircraft were circling overhead so dived. Very soon after diving HE was heard on the starboard bow in addition to the HE of the trawler on the port bow. This new vessel was thought to be a small destroyer type vessel which passed close astern of HMS Una. At 0141 A/18, HMS Una surfaced and proceeded. The destroyer was seen on the port quarter going away and the trawler and her three small consorts were now out of sight. [We have not been able to identify this trawler.] A number of MZ-boats had sailed from Palermo at 1700 A/17 for Sfax but it is doubtful that they could have reached this area in time as their speed was certainly less than 10 knots. The 'small destroyer' sighted was Probably one of the following corvettes: Antilope, Artemide or Gabbiano. These corvettes had sailed from Trapani at 06530 A/17 to carry out A/S search ahead of the Campania convoy, starting at 1430 A/17. However, at 2113 A/17, the corvettes received a signal from Marina Trapani to stop this task and proceed to search for the survivors of the destroyer Bombardiere which had been torpedoed by HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) and reported to have been sunk in position 38°15'N, 11°43'E. From 2345 A/17, the three corvettes were in the area and started their search for survivors. It is probable that one of them was sighted by HMS Una. (2) 19 Jan 1943 At 0530 A/19, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) passed her signal, timed 2332 A/18, to Capt. S.10. It stated that HMS Una would be 24 hours late in arriving off Malta. At 0612 A/19, HMS Una dived to the north-north-west of Linosa Island, in position 36°15'N, 12°44'E. Set course towards Linosa to carry out a periscope reconnaissance of the island. At 1841 A/19, HMS Una surfaced and bombarded the town of Linosa with 20 rounds of high explosive. Point of aim was a large building that looked like a warehouse at the seaside of the town. All rounds were seen to burst on or near the target or within the town. As soon as HMS Una ceased firing and was starting to withdraw, heavy and continuous machine gun firing started. About four guns being used. No rounds fell very close. HMS Una retired to the northward. (2) 20 Jan 1943 Around 1115 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (2) 20 Jan 1943 At 0006 A/20, in position 010° - Linosa lighthouse - 11 miles, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted a darkened object bearing 340°. Course was altered towards. The vessel appeared to be a submarine on an easterly course, but moving very slowly. As HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) were eastbound for Malta this night, it was assumed that this was one of them. At 0020 A/20, HMS P 46 dived after the other submarine had altered course towards. Attempts to exchange identity by S.S./T. were unsuccessful. The other submarine was HMS Una which reported seeing P 46 at 0025 A/20 after which HMS P 46 was seen to dive.
