At 0122 A/20, HMS P 46 surfaced and resumed her original course. At 2350 A/20, HMS P 46 established patrol in the vicinity of Ras Mahmur. (46) 30 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/30, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet, Tunisia. At 2102 A/30, while north-west of Gozo Island, a possible torpedo track was sighted on the starboard side. HMS Una dived and it was thought HE was picked up whilst dived. HMS Una surfaced at 2220 A/30, and proceeded. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. (47) 31 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1737 A/31, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that Allied motor torpedo boats [this were HMMTB 260, HMMTB 264, HMMTB 307, HMMTB 313] will be operating continuously in the Kuriat area from tonight 31st until 0100/4th. They were ordered not to attack submarines. (45) 31 Jan 1943 At 2104 A/31, well to the south-west of Pantelleria, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat sighted about a mile away on the starboard quarter. HE was picked up for about one hour. HMS Una surfaced at 0010 A/1 and continued her passage. (2) 1 Feb 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) damaged the Italian schooners L’Angelo Raffaele (74 GRT) and Nicolo’ Lo Porto (71 GRT) with gunfire in the Gulf of Hammamet, Tunisia in position 36°26'N, 10°43'E. HMS Una had to break of the attack due to gunfire from shore batteries. These schooners had sailed at 0850 A/1 from Bizerte for Sousse. [No further info available.] 1210 A/1 - Surfaced and gunned two 250 tons 2-masted Italian motor schooners anchored off Nabeul jetty. Opening range was 1500 yards bu this was closed to 500 yards. 40 Rounds were fired with the 12-pdr. gun. Two hits were obtained on one schooner and at least 6 on the other. Una was under fire from shore during the whole action. At first this fire from the shore was inaccurate but when an anti-tank gun joined in Una was soon hit twice and one of the gun crew was injured by splinters. When Una dived she grounded at 10 feet and it was necessary to surface and run to seaward for about 10 minutes. During this time she sustained two more hits. 1255 A/1 - Dived and withdrew to seawards. (2) 2 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 0458 A/2, which was passed at 0530 A/2, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) signalled to the Capt. S.10 that a crew member had been injured and medical advice was asked for. At 1215 A/2, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1037 A/2 was received which ordered HMS Una to return to Malta. (2) 3 Feb 1943 At 1115 A/3, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Malta to land the injured crew member and to effect small repairs. At 1600 A/3, HMS Una departed Malta to resume her patrol. She was now ordered to patrol south of the Straits of Messina and off the east coast of Calabria. She was to proceed through position 35°12'N, 15°40'E en-route. 8 Feb 1943 At 1224 A/8, east of Bovalino, Calabria, in approximate position 38°08'N, 16°15'E, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a medium and a small tug passing 1500 yards inshore of HMS Una. They were proceeding to the north-east. A dummy attack was carried out on these enemy vessels. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (2) 9 Feb 1943 At 1415 A/9, in approximate position 38°42'N, 16°42'E, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted four motor drifers about the size of British ML's and armed with one heavy machine gun, proceeding south of Statelli Point. (2) 10 Feb 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Cosala (4294 GRT, former Yugoslavian Serafin Topic, built 1913) near position 38°30'N, 16°40'E. The damaged Italian ship is grounded, declared a total loss and sank during a storm. 0030 A/10 - Received an aircraft reported of one transport and a destroyer proceeding southwards from the Gulf of Taranto. Lt. Martin decided to try to intercept near Punta Stilo. Course was altered accordingly. 0547 A/10 - Dived in position 38°34'N, 16°49'E. 0600 A/10 - In the intended position of Cape Stilo. Nothing was however seen that morning of the expected convoy. Una remained close inshore until about 1500 A/10. During the forenoon a continuous air patrol was patrolling in the area. The convoy reported by the aircraft was most probably the one made up of the transport Petrarca (Italian, 3329 GRT, built 1910) carrying ammunition escorted by the torpedo boat Pegaso. They had sailed from Taranto at 1801 A/9 for Tunis via Palermo. At 0700 A/10, Petrarca came under air attack and ran aground north of Crotone. She was finished off by HMS Una on 15 February 1943. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ 1455 A/10 - Sighted a northbound unladen 4000 tons merchant vessel escorted by one aircraft. Started attack. 1551 A/10 - Fired four torpedoes from 1000 to 1200 yards. One hit was obtained after just over a minute. 1556 A/10 - Sighted the target down by the stern. Una meanwhile withdrew to seaward to reload the torpedo tubes. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ 1958 A/10 - Surfaced in position 38°30'N, 16°56'E, and proceeded towards Cape Alice. (2) 12 Feb 1943 At 0550 A/12, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 39°15'N, 17°14'E and closed the land while dived. During the forenoon HMS Una attempted to gain a lee under Alice Point but a heavy sea was running right up the coast. At 0910 A/12, in position 180° - Alica Point - 6 miles a small motor coaster of about 500 tons was sighted ashore beam on, heading south. This vessel was probably mistaken for a tanker on two occasions by the RAF. It is thought this ship must have suffered a severe bearing in the weather conditions of the past few days. This was the Italian Emilio (191 GRT, 1912) which had ran aground at 1700/11 February near Torre Melissa, south of Alice Point and was a total loss. (2) 13 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1707 A/13, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) to carefully investigate the position 10 miles south of Alice Point where aircraft reported a tanker stopped at 1320 A today. The enemy vessel is probably beached and has vessels standing by protecting her. (48) 13 Feb 1943 At 0648 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 39°23'N, 17°12'E and commenced to close the coast. It was intended to investigate the wreck seen the previous day with a view of torpedoing her if salvage seemed possible. At 0730 A/13, enemy aircraft commenced an intensive search of the area. At 0835 A/13, a Generali-class torpedo boat was sighted which joined the aircraft in it's A/S search. This was the Italian torpedo boat Angelo Bassini which had sailed from Crotone at 0620 A/13 to assist the transport Petrarca which had run aground near Strongoli (Punta Alice area). She reached the area at 0751 A/13 and conducted an A/S search and returned to Crotone at 0830 A/14. At 1300 A/13, an A/S schooner joined in the search which continued until darkness. At one time the torpedo boat passed very near, almost directly overhead. The vessels were not transmitting on Asdic but the impression was that the presence of the submarine was known. The sea was glassy calm during the forenoon and as HMS Una withdrew to the north-eastwards, the enemy A/S vessels moved with her. At 1615 A/13, an Allied air raid on Crotone was observed. At 1645 A/13, a small schooner, probably A/S fitted and in ballast, slowly passed in the direction of Alice Point. At 1847 A/13, HMS Una surfaced and proceeded towards Trionto Point. At 2006 A/13, HMS Una received a signal timed 1707 A/13, in which the Capt. S.10 ordered her to carefully investigate the position 10 miles south of Alice Point where aircraft reported a tanker stopped at 1320 A today. The enemy vessel is probably beached and has vessels standing by protecting her. The schooners sighted on this day may have been part of the group operating from Crotone during the day: Carmelo Solonia (?), V 265 / Cesena (105 GRT, built 1921), Zueca P. (?) and / or Francesco di Gregorio (?). (2) 14 Feb 1943 At 0540 A/14, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 39°18'N, 17°18'E and proceeded submerged to investigate the wreck seen on the 12th. The wind had increased to force 7 during the early hours of the morning, making depth keeping very difficult. At 0840 A/14, the vessel was sighted and she was closed to investigate. She proved to be a standard cargo vessel of about 4000 to 5000 tons painted light and dark grey in stripes. The vessel appeared completely intact though a little down by the stern. She was heading south and was laden. Considered that salvage was comparatively easy and that the vessel should be torpedoed. The prevailing southerly gale made it impossible to do so on this day. At 0920 A/14, HMS Una withdrew to seaward surfacing at 1903 A/14. (2) 15 Feb 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and sank the (already damaged and grounded) Italian transport Petrarca (3329 GRT, built 1910) about 10 nautical miles north of Crotone, Calabria, Italy in position 39°16'N, 17°08'E. 0545 A/15 - Dived in position 39°03'N, 17°19'E and started to close the beached vessel. The area was patrolled by an aircraft, an A/S schooner, an A/S brigantine and an armed yacht. Salvage operations were seen to be in progress. As the sea was flat calm Una was very visible from the air a periscope depth. It was decided to wait to attack after sunset. 1700 A/15 - Closed the target while dived. 1704 A/15 - Fired three torpedoes from about 3000 yards. The torpedoes were seen to run straight to the target but the result could not be seen as depth control was lost soon afterwards and HMS Una hit the bottom. A hit was heard given a running range of 2700 yards. 1820 A/15 - A loud explosion was heard from the direction of the target and it was hoped this was the vessel blowing up. Whilst withdrawing HE was heard to seaward but no counter attack developed. 2359 A/15 - Surfaced and ran to the eastward for an hour before heading for Malta. (Una was to proceed through positions 38°38'N, 17°52'E, 35°20'N, 15°17'E and 35°20'N, 14°18'E on the way back.) (2) 16 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 0505 A/16, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 of the following; 24 hours late. 10th Feb. sank 4000 ton north bound merchant ship near Cape Stilo. P.M./15th torpedoed similar vessel beached south of Alice. One torpedo remains. A weather report was also included in the signal. (49) 19 Feb 1943 Around 1045 A/19, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (2) 1 Mar 1943 Around 1400 A/1, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for the U.K. where she was to refit. She was to pass through position 38°01'N, 11°30'E (west of Marettimo) at 0500 A/3. Further onward routing would be signalled by Capt. S.8. On board were only two MK II torpedoes. In a signal timed 1652 A/1, the Capt. S.8 sent the following instructions; Proceed through the following positions; (A) 38°17'N, 11°10'E at 1300 A/3, (B) 38°17'N, 01°37'E at 1000 A/7, (C) 135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles at 1000 A/8, (D) 180° - Cape de Gata - 5 miles at 0500 A/9th and (E) 180° - Cape Calaburras - 5 miles at 0600 A/10 to arrive at Gibraltar 1100 A/10. Una was not to pass position 'E' before 0600 A/10th owing to Allied A/S patrols. Una was to shift normal W/T watch to Algiers broadcast at 0500 A/3 and to Gibraltar broadcast at 0500 A/8th. Submerged bombing restrictions arranged 10 miles either side of your route from 35 miles ahead to 35 miles astern of your estimated position. Total bombing restrictions arranged on the 10th. For the daily positions of HMS Una during this passage see the map below. (49) 3 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1140 A/3 the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that four destroyers would pass through position 330° - Cape Serrat - 11 miles at 1930 A/3 and proceed through positions 38°08'N, 10°50'E and 38°03'N, 11°22'E thence two destroyers would proceed to Malta and two would return to Bone. This referred to the destroyers HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Loyal (Lt.Cdr. H.E.F. Tweedie, DSC, RN) (which were to return to Bone) and the escort destroyer HMS Easton (Lt. C.W. Malins, RN) and Miaoulis (?) (which were to proceed to Malta). In a signal timed 1437 A/3, the Vice-Admiral Gibraltar informed HMS Una that the Spanish merchant vessels Cristina (2135 GRT, built 1921) and Monte Monjuich (4889 GRT, built 1922) would leave Genoa on the 5th and 6th March respectively for Gibraltar and were not to be molested. (50) 6 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1411 A/6, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was informed by the Capt. S.8 that HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) would departed Gibraltar for Algiers at 1600 A/7 routed through positions 180° - Cape Calaburras - 15 miles at 2125 A/7, 36°37'N, 04°00'W at 0230 A/8, 180° - Cape de Gata - 15 miles at 0013 A/9 and 135° - Cape Palos - 15 miles at 2143 A/9. In a signal timed 1544 A/6, the Flag Officer Gibrsaltar, informed HMS Una that two Spanish submarines are exercising in vicinity of Cartagena March 9th to 11th as follows: General Mola, trials by day off Cape Tinos. C 4 in Masarron area torpedo firing exercises. (51) 7 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1817 A/7, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) were informed that HMS Laforey (Capt. R.M.J. Hutton, DSO, RN) and HMS Lookout (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) left Algiers at 1044 A/7th to proceed to Gibraltar at 22 knots. HMS Tartar (Cdr. St J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN) and ORP Blyskawica (Kmdr.ppor. (Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski) would leave Philippeville at 2000 A/7 to proceed to Gibraltar at approximately 28 knots. All are informed of your movements. (51) 9 Mar 1943 At 0322 A/9, north-west of Algiers, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) and took avoiding action. Apparently HMS Unshaken did not see HMS Una. (2) 10 Mar 1943 Around 1100 A/10, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Malta. (2) 12 Mar 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) conducted D/G trials at Gibraltar. (2) 13 Mar 1943 Around 1245 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Gibraltar for Holy Loch. On the 11th, two additional torpedoes (Mk. VIII's) had been embarked in addition to the two MK. II torpedoes that had been embarked at Malta. For the daily positions of HMS Una during this passage see the map below. (2) 24 Mar 1943 Around 1400 A/24, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) joined HMS P 556 (Lt.Cdr. F.H. Sherwood, RCNVR) and USS Blackfish (T/Cdr. J.F. Davidson, USN) which came from Falmouth. They were escorted by HMS Lundy (T/Lt. S.J. Lavis, RNR). Around 1820 A/24, HMS Lundy parted company and shortly afterwards HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) joined. (52) 26 Mar 1943 Around 1300 A/26, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Gibraltar. (2) 28 Mar 1943 Around 1715 A/28, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Holy Loch for Blyth. She was escorted by HMS Alecto (Lt.Cdr. H.A.L. Marsham, RN) until 0915 A/30 when HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) took over the escort. (2) 31 Mar 1943 Around 1620 A/31, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Blyth from Holy Loch. At Blyth she is to be taken in hand for refit. (2) 4 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Blyth. (53) 5 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) ended her refit at Blyth. (53) 7 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted a basin dive trial at Blyth followed by D/G trials. (53) 9 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted diving and gunnery trials off Blyth. (53) 10 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted diving trials off Blyth. (53) 11 Aug 1943 Around 1745 A/11, HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN), HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Uther (Lt. P.S. Beale, RN) departed Blyth for Holy Loch. They were escorted by HMS ML 300 (T/Lt. F.C.R. Piesse, RNVR) until Dundee where HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) took over the escort. (53) 14 Aug 1943 Around 1330 A/14, HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) arrived at Rothesay from Blyth. HMS Una, being a member of the 7th Submarine Flotilla, was to be used for training from now on. (53) 16 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted independent exercises in Loch Striven. (53) 18 Aug 1943 Around 2000 A/18, HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay for Fishguard. (62) 16 Mar 1944 HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) is recalled from Campbeltown to Rothesay to take on board fuel and provisions as she is sent on patrol off Norway as the British think that the German battleship Tirpitz is going to leave Northern Norway for repairs in Germany. HMS Una departed for Lerwick at 0830 A/16 together with HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) and HrMs Zeehond (Lt.Cdr. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN). At 1300 A/16, off Sanda Island, they were joined by their escort, the corvette HMCS Camrose (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.R. Pavillard, RCNR) which remained with them until around 1545 A/17. HMS Safari and HMS Una continued onwards in company towards Lerwick while HrMs Zeehond proceeded independently towards Lerwick. (2) 18 Mar 1944 Around 0850 A/18, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) arrived at Lerwick for fuel. At 1135 A/18, she departed for 20th her war patrol. Patrol area was the south-west coast of Norway off Egersund passing through position 60°09'N, 03°25'E en-route. For the daily positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below. HMS Una 20th war patrol click here for bigger map (2) 21 Mar 1944 As it has become clear that the German battleship Tirpitz is not on the move back to Germany, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) is recalled from patrol in the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 2308 A/20 which was received at 0022 A/21. (2) 23 Mar 1944 Around 1605 A/23, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) ended her 20th war patrol at Lerwick. (2) 24 Mar 1944 Around 0210 A/24, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) departed Lerwick for Rothesay. She made the passage together with HMS Truant (Lt. Return to the Allied Warships section
The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (10)
16 Feb 1942 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 5th war patrol see the map below.

17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (9)
20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1205 B/20 Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

At 2000 B/20 proceed as follows; HMS P 34 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E to Area K off Kerkenah. HMS Una through positions 33°20'N, 13°45'E and 33°20'N, 12°55'E to Area N south of 33°45'W and west of 12°55'E. HMS P 39 was to operate in area L, 32°10'N, 15°00'W - 000° - 40 - 50.
Three more submarines will sail shortly to concentrate again with HMS Una and HMS P 39 in the vicinity of Khoms.
In a signal timed 2207 B/20 they were ordered by Capt. S.10 to return to their previous patrol positions by 0001 B/23. (9)

20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow.

The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).
At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron.
At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay.

At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23.

At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22.
At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24.

At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy.

Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23.

Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23.

The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24.
The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (9)

21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (9)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (9)

23 Feb 1942 While searching for the reported enemy convoy, depth charges were dropped near HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) between 1000 B/23 and 1130 B/23. In all about 70 were dropped with a range varying between 200 yards and 3 nautical miles. No damage was caused except for a few broken lights.
Earlier that day, at 0920 B/23, a small diesel trawler had been sighted. The enemy passed one mile to the northward of the submarine. Enemy course was 230°.
All this was near Una's patrol position which was 32°48'N, 14°11'E. During the time HMS Una was kept down it appeared that the convoy passed about 5 miles to the northward of her.
At 1200 B/23, two destroyer were seen to return to the area at high speed but they did not drop depth charges.
At 1238 B/23, a destroyer passed astern of HMS Una at close range while proceeding to the eastward at over 30 knots.
At 2057 B/23, HMS Una which already had retired a bit to the northwards left patrol to return to Malta. (8)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (9)
25 Feb 1942 At 0730 A/25, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)

5 Mar 1942 At 1215 B/5, while at Malta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was straddled by two heavy calibre bombs. One landed in the creek about 150 yards on the starboard beam and the other landed on the shore about 200 yards on the port beam. No apparent damage was caused. (11)

6 Mar 1942 At 1205 B/5, while at Malta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) the submarine base was attacked once more and a stick of bombs exploded near the submarine moorings. A fuel lighter was hit and exploded. HMS Una was not damaged though. (11)

7 Mar 1942 At 2200 B/7, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol near Kerkennah no.4 buoy until A.M. on 10 March and then in area 'K'.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below.
9 Mar 1942 At 2102 B/9, on surfacing in position 34°45'N, 11°58'E, after a day of submerged patrol, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) set course for area 'K' between Mahedia, Lampedusa and Kerkennah.
At 2120 B/9, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1810 B/9 was received. Course was set to try to intercept a 14 knot convoy around 0700 B/10. (12)

9 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1745 B/9, the Vice Admiral Malta informed HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, ORP) of the following; Force K will sweep north from position 36°05'N, 11°50'E between 0200 B/10 and 0300 B/10.

In a follow up signal the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to these three submarines; My 1745 B/9. P 31 patrol in position 35°27'N, 12°23'E from 0400 B/10 to intercept a 14 knot ship on approximate course 140° then return to arrive Grand Harbour.
Sokol keep west of 11°40'E until 0400 B/10 then return to northern route.
Una remain on that route south of 35°30'N.

[The 14-knot ship referred to was the 14-knot transport Luciano Manara (8396 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce which sailed from Trapani at 1600 B/9-1700 B/9. Shortly after leaving Trapani, Centauro had defects which forced her to return to Trapani at 1730 B/9. From 2125 B/9, the convoy came under air attack and Luciano Manara was damaged by RAF torpedo bombers (three Swordfish and three Albacores, one Albacore cashed on landing on return to Malta) at 2310 B/9. She dropped anchor at Pantelleria at 0053 B/10 and then sailed again at 0950 B/10 to return to Palermo where she arrived at 0135 B/11. (ULTRA source, see DEFE3/939/2, pp. 1091-1092)]

[Force K, made up of the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. M.S. Slattery, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyer HMS Kingston (Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta around 2000 B/9 to intercept the above convoy. As the convoy broke off its passage they failed to find it and they returned to Malta around 0900 B/10.] (13)
10 Mar 1942 While attempting to attack an northbound escorted merchant ship HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is detected and depth charged by one of the escorts.
0548 B/10 - Dived in position 35°12'N, 11°50'E which was the anticipated 0700 B/10 position of the expected 14 knot southbound convoy.

[The submarine had been informed by S.10's signal timed 1810 B/9 of a 14-knot convoy and ordered to intercept it at 0700/10 in 35°19'N, 11°50'E (ULTRA source, see DEFE3/939/2, pp. 1091-1092). This was the 14-knot transport Luciano Manara (8396 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce which sailed from Trapani at 1600 B/9-1700 B/9. Shortly after leaving Trapani, Centauro had defects which forced her to return to Trapani at 1730 B/9. From 2125 B/9, the convoy came under air attack and Luciano Manara was damaged by RAF torpedo bombers at 2310 B/9. She dropped anchor at Pantelleria at 0053 B/10 and then sailed again at 0950 B/10 to return to Palermo where she arrived at 0135 B/11.]
0830 B/10 - No sign of the southbound convoy [see above]. Set course to intercept a 11 knot northbound convoy in its anticipated 1100 B/10 position.
1015 B/10 - In the anticipated interception position, 35°22'N, 11°40'E.
1130 B/10 - An aircraft was sighted to the westward.
1138 B/10 - Heard HE bearing 225°.
1140 B/10 - Near position 35°24'N, 11°39'E sighted a medium sized merchant vessel bearing 225°. Range was 5000 yards. Enemy course 005°. Started attack.
1145 B/10 - The target was seen to be escorted by two destroyers and an armed trawler. Also two aircraft were overhead.
1152 B/10 - The target zigged towards placing Una in a very favourable attack position.
1154 B/10 - The target was seen to turn away and the trawler was seen to hoist a red flag and turned towards.
1155 B/10 - The trawler was still approaching and Lt. Norman was forced to break off the attack as Una had been detected. Una now went deep.
1159 B/10 - The counter attack started.
1233 B/10 - Between 1159 and 1233 hours 16 depth charges were dropped but none were close. Una meanwhile withdraw to the south.

This convoy was most likely the one made up of the German transport Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the destroyer Bersagliere, the torpedo boat Castore and the tug Instancabile (former Yugoslav salvage vessel Spasilac, 740 tons, built 1929, captured in 1941). They sailed from Tripoli at 1900 B/9-2100 B/9 and arrived at Naples at 1725 B/11, except Instancabile which went to Lampedusa (see below) and Bersagliere which had been detached to Palermo. At 1157 B/10, the escorting aircraft gave a submarine alarm and Bersagliere went on to attack with eight depth charges and ordered Castore to hunt the submarine. The torpedo boat dropped eight depth charges but was getting echoes from several directions; then, in a second run, dropped another four depth charges. At 1300 B/10, Castore abandoned the hunt and rejoined the convoy. At 1305 B/10, the aircraft gave a new alarm and Bersagliere dropped seven depth charges (this must have been a bogus sighting). In both instances, the destroyer and the torpedo boat did not detect the submarine and thus attacked haphazardly. Unfortunately, Bersagliere report does not give either positions, but at 0810 B/10, the convoy was in view of Kerkennah buoy No.4 so the convoy must have been reasonably near HMS Una three hours later. (8)

12 Mar 1942 At 0830 B/12, while patrolling near Mahdia, Tunisia in approximate position 35°33'N, 11°13'E, a Vichy-French patrol vessel, similar to the British PC 74, was seen proceeding southwards just inside territorial waters. The French flag was painted on the ships side. (8)

13 Mar 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) sank the Italian schooner Maria Immacolata (248 GRT, cargo of sugar and general cargo bound for Tripoli) with gunfire 7.8 nautical miles bearing 135° off Mahedia, Tunisia. A second schooner, Maria Camali, was in company at a distance but was not seen by the submarine.
1013 B/13 - Sighted two fishing boats to the northward.
1140 B/13 - Sighted a 3-masted schooner bearing 040°, range 6000 yards, enemy course 130°.
1145 B/13 - Gave chase to this schooner.
1218 B/13 - Surfaced in position 35°26'N, 11°11'E, 60° on the Port bow of the schooner at a range of 600 yards. Engaged with the gun but it misfired.
1222 B/13 - The schooner turned away towards the coast. Gave chase and engaged it with the Lewis gun.
1223 B/13 - The crew of the schooner abandoned ship after firing one round with a rifle. The defect on the 12pdr. gun was made good and fire was opened from 80 yards.
1243 B/13 - The schooner sank in position 135°, Mahedia Light, 7.8 nautical miles. Closed the survivors in their boat. They were not picked up and ordered to make for the coast 8 nautical miles away.
1255 B/13 - Dived and withdrew to the east. During the action 30 round were fired with the 12pdr. gun and 500 with the Lewis gun. (8)

14 Mar 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) attacked an escorted transport in approximate position 35°29'N, 12°15'E (west of Lampedusa) with three torpedoes which were fired from long range. No hits were obtained.

This was the German transport Trapani (1855 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cantore on passage from Tripoli to Trapani. The transport sighted four torpedo tracks (only three had been fired) and took evasive action while a Savoia 79 bomber dropped a green smoke float to mark the position of the submarine. Cantore rushed to the position and dropped ten depth charges, the first two set at 25 meters and the remainder at 50 meters charges (in Una only eight explosions were heard so probably two failed to explode). By precaution, Trapani was directed to Pantelleria before resuming her passage on the 15th.
1152 B/14 - Went deep as it appeared a systematic air seach was going on in the area.
1203 B/14 - Heard HE on bearing 280°. It was thought this was a patrol vessel out of Lampedusa.

1205 B/14 - Deciphered Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 B/10 (1 merchant ship, 1 destroyer in position 165° - Lampione Island - 36 miles on course 350°, 6 knots at 0954 B/14) and realised that the HE was a convoy that was reported in this signal. Came to periscope depth and turned towards.
1213 B/14 - At periscope depth. Sighted a transport of about 4000 tons and a destroyer bearing 285° at a range of 5000 to 6000 yards. Enemy course was 015°. Closed on a 115° track.
1223 B/14 - In position 190° - Lampione Island - 7 miles fired three torpedoes at the transport from a range of 5000 to 6000 yards.
1227 B/14 - Heard a loud explosion. This may have been either a bomb exploding or a torpedo hit at a running range of 6000 yards.
1232 B/14 - A counter attack was carried out by the destroyer which dropped eight single depth charges.
1330 B/14 - HE of the destroyer faded out. (12)

14 Mar 1942 At 1400 B/14, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) set course to the eastward in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1051 B/13 which ordered her to patrol in position 37°43'N, 15°33'E (South of the Straits of Messina) as of dawn on the 17th proceeding through position 35°20'N, 12°35'E at 2000 B/14 and then through position 36°06'N, 14°03'E. (12)

16 Mar 1942 At 0621 B/16, near Cape Passaro, Sicily, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) picked up HE (diesel) on bearing 290°. Una turned towards. Periscope visibility was 2000 to 3000 yards. Nothing was sighted.
At 0625 B/16, there was still nothing in sight.
At 0633 B/16, the HE faded out. Periscope visibility was 4000 yards at that moment. It is thought this may have been a submarine or motor torpedo boat returning to Augusta.

We have been unable to place an enemy submarine in this area at this time but the German motor torpedo boats (E-boats) S-54, S-31, S-35, S-36 and S-61 passed through this area between 0600 B/16 - 0630 B/16 on their return to Augusta (arrived around 0745 B/16) from a minelaying operation off Malta. (8)
17 Mar 1942 At 2040 B/17, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) picked up HE thought to be of a destroyer / torpedo boat on bearing 020°.
At 2105 B/17, the HE faded out on bearing 070°. Nothing had been seen of the source of the HE so HMS Una surfaced in position 37°40'N, 15°35'E.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (8)

18 Mar 1942 At 2105 B/18, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0520 B/18. Course was set to comply. She was now to patrol on a line 060°-240° between position M and N. (37°43'N, 15°33'E and 37°47.N, 15°45'E respectively). (8)

19 Mar 1942 At 2055 B/19, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced in position 37°40'N, 15°31'E. Course was now set to leave patrol and return to Malta. She was to proceed through position 37°00'N, 15°36'E to arrive off Malta at 0645 B/21. (8)
21 Mar 1942 At 1035 B/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
31 Mar 1942 Around 2130 B/31, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol South of the Strait of Messina.
2 Apr 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) arrived in her patrol area between Cape Spartivento and Cape Bruzzano. (8)

4 Apr 1942 At 1300 C/4, east-south-east of Cape Spartivento, in approximate position 37°54'N, 16°10'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted two minesweepers proceeding eastwards along the coast. Half an hour later they turned back to the westwards. (8)
5 Apr 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Ninetto G. (5355 GRT, built 1913) about 12 nautical miles south of Capo dell'Armi in position 37°45'N, 15°41'E.

The above mentioned Ninetto G. was in convoy together with the transport Veloce (Italian, 5437 GRT, built 1911). They were proceeding from Taranto to Messina escorted by auxiliary Morosini and the auxiliary submarine chaser Ciprus.

Following the sinking the torpedo boat Orsa equipped with the German S-Gerät (sonar) was ordered to the scene and hunted the submarine in the evening, dropping two pattern of nine depth charges, followed by two more. A large oil slick was observed and the submarine was believed destroyed. Orsa remained on the scene until the next afternoon when she dropped two more depth charges before leaving the scene on the morning of the 7th as she was relieved by the torpedo boat Circe.
Ninetto G. had a crew of thirty. There were twenty-eight survivors (three of them were wounded). Two of the crew were missing.
0555 C/5 - Heard HE bearing 090°.
0559 C/5 - Sighted a destroyer bearing 085° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 300°.
0600 C/5 - Dived in position 37°43'N, 15°54'E, as Una was up moon from the enemy. The destroyer could not be seen through the periscope.
0618 C/5 - HE faded.
This was probably either the destroyer Antonio da Noli or Emanuele Pessagno which sailed from Tripoli respectively at 0930 B/4 and 0852 B/4 and arrived at Messina at 0730 B/5 and 0740 B/5.
1140 C/5 - Sighed smoke bearing 040°.
1155 C/5 - Sighted two merchant ships in single line ahead. Started attack.
1210 C/5 - Sighted that the merchant vessels were escorted by two yachts.
1254 C/5 - In position 37°45'N, 15°41'E fired four torpedoes at the 2nd ship of the line from 3000 yards. This ship was thought o be a merchant ship of 7000-8000 tons. Two hits were obtained.
1304 C/5 - The ship was heard to sink.
1315 C/5 - The counter attack started. Una meanwhile withdrew to the souh-east.
1318 C/5 - A depth charge exploded quite close causing some minor damage.
1330 C/5 - The counter attack ended. In all 14 depth charges were dropped.
Between 1345 C/5 and 1420 C/5 - Six more depth charges were dropped some distance away.
Between 2100 C/5 and 2400 C/5 - About sixty depth charges in patterns of six were dropped at intervals to the north-westward in the vicinity of the attack.
2230 C/5 - Una surfaced in position 37°35'N, 16°08'E. Depth charging was going on about 15 miles away. Commenced to withdraw to the eastward. (8)
7 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1131 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN);
HMS Una was to patrol in position 37°42'N, 15°37'E from 2301 B/7 to 0531 B/8. HMS P 34 was to patrol in position 37°00'N, 16°14'E from 0259 B/8 to 0731 B/8.
In a follow up signal timed 2135 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed both submarines that Wellington aircraft from Malta will search and attack enemy shipping after midnight.

This was to try to intercept the BETA-convoy which was made up of the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare which sailed from Naples at 0130 B/7-0208 B/7. The Italian torpedo boat Orsa had sailed from Messina at 1745 B/5 for an A/S sweep and attacked a submarine at 2010 B/5 in position 200° - Cape Spartivento - 3 miles and claimed it sunk. She was then ordered to reinforce the escort of the Ankara. At 0415 B/7, the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza departed from Messina to join Ankara. At 0800 B/7, the Italian torpedo boat Lince sailed from Messina to reinforce the escort and joined at 1301 B/7, followed by Orsa at 1336 B/7 and shortly after by Giuseppe Dezza. Ankara, Antoniotto Usodimare, Giuseppe Dezza and Orsa arrived at Messina at 1728-1750 B/7. Lince and two MAS boats were on an A/S search, the torpedo boat returning to Messina only at 0300 B/8. Ankara, Antoniotto Usodimare and Lince sailed again at 2152 B/7-2330 B/7. At 0022 B/8, Ankara and Antoniotto Usodimare accidentally collided in position 37°55'20"N, 15°25'10"E. The three ships returned to Messina at 0422 B/8-0426 B/8. Ankara, escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince, sailed from Messina at 2150 B/8. At 0837 B/10, the Italian torpedo boat Pallade met them to pilot them in. They arrived at Benghazi at 1145 B/10. (14)

7 Apr 1942 At 1955 C/7, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) was to the south-south-east of Cape Spartivento, in approximate position 37°51'N, 16°06'E, when a transport escorted by a destroyer were sighted at a distance of 4-5 nautical miles. HMS Una tried to close to less than 3 miles but had to abandon the attack at 2105 C/7 when this it was apparent the range could not be closed any further.

This was the Italian transport Arlesiana (5702 GRT, built 1915) escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba. They had sailed from Taranto at 8 knots at 1510 B/6 and arrived at Catania at 0120 B/8. (8)

8 Apr 1942 At 0727 C/7, south of dell'Armi, in approximate position 37°44'N, 15°46'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted the mast and funnels of two cruisers bearing 245°. Enemy course was 320° at a speed (by revs) of 25 knots. Range was 4-5 miles. HMS Una attempted to close to attack range but half an hour later the approach had to be broken off and the cruisers disappeared from sight.

This were the battleship Caio Duilio and the light cruiser Muzio Attendolo screened by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Malocello, Nicolò Zeno and Ugolino Vivaldi. They had sailed from Taranto at 1600 B/7-1725 B/7. They were later reinforced by the torpedo boats Circe and Orsa which sailed from Messina at 0300 B/8-0340 B/8. They arrived at Messina at 0812 B/8-1027 B/8, except Antoniotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi, which were detached at 0812 B?8-0830 B/8 and continued on to Trapani where they arrived at 1820 B/8 while Antonio Pigafetta and Nicolò Zeno were detached to proceed to Reggio Calabria, arriving at 0900 B/8-0915 B/8.
At 1430 C/8, two funnels with red cross markings were sighted bearing 020° at a range of 5 miles.
At 1435 C/8, this vessel was identified as the repatriation ship Duilio (Italian, 24281 GRT, built 1923) which was expected though the ship was 48 hours ahead of schedule.
Duilio had sailed from Trieste at 0900 B/6 at a speed of 16.5 knots and arrived at Gibraltar on 11 April to pick up Italian nationals from East Africa which were to be repatriated. (8)

8 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1046 B/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was ordered to remain on patrol as in Capt. S.10's singal timed 1131 B/7 until A.M. on the 9th. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was ordered to proceed direct to the area off Cape Santa Maria di Leuca off the Gulf of Taranto. (14)
8 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1914 B/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was informed that she was reported by enemy aircraft at 1846 B/8. (14)

10 Apr 1942 At 0645 C/10, south of the Straits of Messina, in approximate position 37°42'N, 15°33'E, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted a destroyer crossing her bow at a range of 800 yards. Enemy course was 110° at 29 knots.
We have been unable to find a destroyer/torpedo boat in this area at that time/date.

At 1135 C/10, on returning to periscope depth in approximate position 37°40'N, 15°53'E, sighted a 4000 ton tanker and escort bearing 080° at a range of only 700 yards. No HE was picked up. Turned hard to starboard at 1138 C/10 and went to 50 feet to avoid being run down and also to attempt to turn and fire at close range on a 170° track. [No course and speed of the enemy given in the patrol report or logbook.]
At 1148 C/10, HMS Una was on a 175° track. Range was now about 3500 yards and the attack was broken off. It was later discovered that the Asdic was defective.

This were most likely the Italian naval tanker Marte< (2502 GRT, built 1892) escorted by the destroyer Turbine which had sailed from Taranto at 9,5 knots at 0810 B/9-0900 B/9. The auxiliary Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940) had sailed from Messina at 0210 B/10 to reinforce the escort. They arrived at Messina at 1500 B/10-1530 B/10. (8)

11 Apr 1942 At 0040 C/11, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1349 B/10 which ordered her to leave patrol through positions 36°25'N, 15°27'E and 36°05'N, 14°50'E to arrive off Marsamxett Harbour P.M. on the 12th. Course was set for a position to the south-east of Augusta with a view to intercept enemy submarine arriving or leaving at dawn.
At 0620 C/11, HMS Una dived in position 37°03'N, 15.40'E.
At 1245 C/11, HMS Una was in position 130° - Cape Murro di Proco - 5 miles. No enemy submarines had been encountered so course was set to the southward. (8)
12 Apr 1942 At 1345 B/12, a rather close heavy bomb explosion was felt by HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN).
Around 1955 B/12, HMS Una ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
18 Apr 1942 At 1704 B/18, while laying on he bottom of the harbour at Malta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is near missed by a bomb causing many minor defects, most could by repaired quickly. (15)
19 Apr 1942 Around 0450 B/19, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in area 'T' to the west of Tripoli.
For the daily positions of HMS Una during this patrol see the map below.

20 Apr 1942 At 1405 B/20, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1053 B/20, which stated the following; Ref. sailing orders cancel area T. Proceed through position 35°40'N, 12°50'E to Area J, west of 11°55'E between 35°35'N. and 36°05'N. Course was set to comply with these orders. (12)

21 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1025 B/21, the Capt. S.10's sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Adjust patrols to be in vicinity line 000° through 35°40'N, 11°49'E after noon 23/4. Further orders will follow. (14)
21 Apr 1942 At 2130 B/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) surfaced in position 36°08'N, 11°49'E and set course to the westward to patrol to the north of Kuriat the following day. (8)

22 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1031 B/22, referring to his signal timed 1025 B/21, the Capt. S. 10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) that two 14-knot escorted merchant ships may be encountered proceeding north on meridian 11°49'E crossing 34°30'N about 1400 B/23. At 2030 B/23, HMS Una was extend her patrol area east to 12°50'E and north to 36°10'N.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) en-route from Tripoli (departed 1935 B/22-2040 B/22) at 14.5 knots for Naples escorted by the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Centauro and Pallade.

At about 1630 B/23-1747 B/23, about 30 miles north of Pantelleria, there was a change of escort with another convoy proceeding to Tripoli: This convoy was made up of the transports Gino Allegri (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1941) and Agostino Bertani (Italian, 8329 GRT, built 1941) and had sailed from Naples at 2245 B/22-2330 B/22, proceeding at 15.5 knots, escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and the Italian torpedo boat Cantore.
The information was once again based on an ULTRA source (see DEFE3/753/486, frame 490 and DEFE3/754, frames 8, 78). (14)
22 Apr 1942 At 2120 B/22, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) surfaced in position 35°54'N, 11°07'E and set course to the eastward to comply with the orders in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1025 B/21. (8)

23 Apr 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) patrolled in the vicinity of position 35°42'N, 11°49'E during this day but nothing was sighted except aircraft patrols P.M. The expected northbound convoy was not sighted. (8)

25 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1232 B/25 the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN); Reconnaissance suggests two important 14 knots southbound escorted ships approaching position 35°46'N, 12°09'E on approximate course 165° about 0700 B/27. Adjust patrol according to moon conditions to intercept before that time on line 345° from position given as far as 36°30'N. Other traffic may be encountered later in vicinity and may pass between Lampion and Lampdeusa.
['Reconnaissance suggests' in this case actually means information from 'ULTRA', codebreaking of enemy signals. The convoy was not yet at sea at this moment see file DEFE3/754, frame 82.]

This signal referred to the convoy made up of the transports Monreale (Italian, 5323 GRT, built 1941) and Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) proceeding from Naples (departure around 0240 B/26-0330 B/26) to Tripoli. On departure from Naples they were escorted by the Italian destroyers Premuda, Lanzerotto Malocello. At 1125 B/26, the Italian torpedo boat Castore sailed from Palermo to reinforce the escort near position 38°22'N, 12°09.5'E. At 1635 B/26, Unione’s steering gear broke down and she was detached to Palermo escorted by Castore they arrived at 2322 B/26. The remaining ships arrived at Tripoli at 2031 B/27-2055 B/27. (14)

26 Apr 1942 At 1200 B/26, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) which is patrolling off Ras Dimass, Tunisia, sets course to the north-east to comply with the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1232 B/25 and 1005 B/26.

This last signal stated the following; My 1232 B/25. Alternative route appears to be a course of 185° to arrive in position 36°01'N, 11°49'E about 0530 B/27. Patrol vicinity of position 36°20'N, 11°55'E until dawn 27/4. Anticipate enemy pass that position 0400 B to westwards or 0430 B to eastwards. Do not approach Pantellaria closer than 20 miles and retire if possible to S.S.E. on leaving patrol position in search of other targets. (16)
27 Apr 1942 At 0300 B/27, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived in position 36°20'N, 11°55'E (south of Pantelleria) to intercept the southbound convoy. There was a bright moon and the sea was calm.
At 0410 B/27, flares were dropped and enemy AA fire was sighted bearing 125° approximately 12 miles away in approximate position 36°08'N, 12°08'E. Una set course to close.
At 0424 B/27, an underwater explosion was heard.
At 0530 B/27, HMS Una dived in position 36°10'N, 12°01'E.
At 0530 B/27, HMS Una arrived in the position of the aircraft attack on the convoy. Nothing was sighted and course was set towards Lampione Island.

At 1308 B/27, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1001 B/27 was received which stated the following; Patrol vicinity of position 36°09'N, 11°51'E from 0001 B/28 in anticipation of a northbound transport at about 0430 B on course 005°. Aircraft may attack, before midnight. Leave position not later than 0800 B/28 and return to Malta through positions 35°42'N, 12°50'E and 36°11'N, 14°21'E to enter Marsamxett at 2030 B/30.
Course was set to the north-west to comply with the orders in this signal.

This signal referred to the German transport Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) and the Italian transport Reginaldo Giuliani (6837 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Pallade and Centauro. They had sailed from Tripoli at 1137 B/27-1300 B/27. At 0634 B/28, off Pantelleria, Folgore and Centauro left the convoy to reinforce the escort of a southbound convoy made up of the Italian tanker Saturno (5022 GRT, built 1914) and the Italian transport San Luigi (4356 GRT, built 1905) escorted by the torpedo boats Cigno, Castore and Lince which had sailed from Palermo at 0955 B/27-1114 B/27 for Tripoli. Cigno left this convoy to join Pallade, escorting Reichenfels and Reginaldo Giuliani to Naples. The bad weather forced Reginaldo Giuliani and Pallade to stop at Trapani at 1430 B/28-0930 B/29. They arrived at Naples at 1315 B/30. Reichenfels escorted by Cigno arrived at Palermo at 2027 B/29. In the meantime, the Saturno convoy continued to Tripoli but, at 0831 B/28, Lince indicated that she had seakeeping difficulties and was detached to Pantelleria. At 0905 B/28, San Luigi could not make more than 6 knots in the heavy seas, and Centauro was ordered to escort her to Pantelleria and then rejoin the convoy. However, at 1035 B/28, Saturno and her escorts also turned back to take refuge in Pantelleria as the intense scirocco made the proceedings quite difficult. At 0202 B/29, Saturno escorted by Folgore, Castore, Centauro (Lince remained in Pantelleria for temporary repairs) sailed from Pantelleria. At 0820 B/30, they were met by the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari which piloted them to Tripoli where they arrived at 1138 B/30-1225 B/30. (16)

28 Apr 1942 At 0805 B/28, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0504 B/28, which stated the following; Southbound tanker damaged by aircraft in position 37°17'N, 11°42'E at 0045 B/28 may be proceeding at less than 12 knots. Remain last patrol position ordered until 2030 B/28 then return to Malta as previously ordered.
Course was set to proceed to position 36°10'N, 11°50'E to intercept this damaged southbound tanker. At 0900 B/28, HMS Una was back in position but no damaged tanker was encountered.

This signal must have referred to the tanker Saturno (Italian, 5029 GRT, built 1914) which was in convoy with the transport San Luigi (4419 GRT, built 1905). They were on passage from Palermo to Tripoli escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Cigno, Castore and Lince. Due to the bad weather contitions encountered the convoy returned to Pantelleria to continue it's passage at a later date. Due to the convoy returning it was possibly thought the tanker was damaged [See also the event for 27 April 1942.] The convoy was attacked by a single torpedo bomber (Albacore) at 0048 B/28 20 miles north of Pantelleria but suffered no damage. The torpedo boats made smoke to shield the convoy and San Luigi fired a burst of machine gun fire. (16)
30 Apr 1942 At 2035 B/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)

4 May 1942 Around 0430 B/4, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Malta for Alexandria. She was to leave Malta by the north-east searched channel (71° from St. Elmo Light for 18 miles) and then through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E around 0001 B/5 and through position 34°50'N, 20°00'E about 1400 B/7. Further instructions would be signalled by Capt. S.1.
Due to the heavy bombing of Malta the 10th Submarine Fotilla was evacuated from Malta to Alexandria. Una carried 14 passengers and stores.
[No log is available so no map can be displayed.] (17)

5 May 1942 In a signal timed 1810 C/5, Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) to proceed through position 32°23'N, 28°46'N to arrive at the entrance to the Alexandria searched channel at 0530 C/13. (18)
13 May 1942 Around 0745 C/13, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived at Alexandria.
[It is likely that during May 1942 HMS Una was docked but no log of this month is available so we can not be sure.] (17)
31 May 1942 Around 1345 C/31, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) left Alexandria for exercises. She returned to harbour around 0650 C/1. The exercises had included night exercises.

6 Jun 1942 Around 1730 C/6, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Alexandria for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to give cover during convoy operations to Malta and took position 'A' in 35°37'N, 18°44'E, the southernmost point of a patrol line (bearing 050° at 12 miles intervals) with HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) in position 'B', HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) in position 'D'. The patrol line was to cover the convoy proceeding to Malta from Alexnadria during Operation VIGOROUS).
13 Jun 1942 At 0500 C/13, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) arrived in patrol position 'A' (35°37'N, 18°44'E).
At 0730 C/13, an Italian hospital ship passed 1500 yards off track. Enemy course was 350° at 12 knots.
This was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926). She had sailed from Derna at 1100 B/12 and arrived at Naples at 1300 B/14. (17)
15 Jun 1942 At 0630 C/15, while still in position 'A' (35°37'N, 18°44'E), HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) heard a heavy explosion to the north-east.
At 0730 C/15, a large column of black smoke was seen on bearing 050° at an estimated range of about 15 to 20 miles. HMS Una set course to proceed towards at speed.
At 0830 C/15, sighted that a white smoke screen was being laid to the north of the column of smoke. The range did not seem to be closing.
At 0900 C/15, the smoke was dwindling. Still there was no ship in sight so set course to return to the patrol position. (17)

17 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1833 C/17, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to proceeded towards Taranto with despatch and that additional orders would follow.
In a follow up signal timed 2015 C/17 the following orders were signalled; My 1835 C/17 adjust speed and time leaving present patrol area so as to arrive by dawn 20th.

In yet another signal timed 2101 C/17 the following orders were signalled; Establish patrol by dawn 20th as follows; HMS Thorn in the vicinity of position 40°04'N, 17°13'E HMS Thrasher in the vicinity of position 40°07'N, 17°26'E. HMS Porpoise in the vicinity of position 35°01'N, 16°29'E. 10th Submarine Flotilla was to patrol line; 4 - 39°47'N 17°17'E - 7 miles - 055°. HMS Una in position 'A', 39°47'N, 17°17'E. Remainder in present relative positions. [thus HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 in positions 'B', 'C' and 'D' respectively.] Proceed direct to new position diving at dawn 20th on line 247° from Cape Maria di Leuca. (19)

19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1109 C/19, the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to return to Alexandria forthwith through following positions to arrive as stated; HMS Porpoise through positions 35°22'N, 18°11'E and 32°21'N, 28°01'E to arrive at dawn on the 22nd. HMS Thorn through positions 37°21'N, 20°30'E, 35°42'N, 21°57(?)'E and 32°08'N, 28°57'E to arrive at dawn on the 25th. The four boats of the 10th submarine flotilla were to cross Latitude 34.59'N in the following Longitudes; HMS Una in 21°28'E, HMS P 31 in 22°04'E, HMS P 34 in 22°40'E and HMS P 35 in 23°18'E. [More positions to proceed through are given in the signal but it contains several errors]. The four boats were to arrive off the boom before 2030 C/27 or after 0730 C/28. (19)

19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2059 C/19, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN); Establish patrol line forthwith 5 - 38°16'N, 19°47'E - 8 - 150° in the order HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34, HMS P 35, HMS Thrasher. HMS Una in position 38°16', 19°47'E. HMS Thorn proceeding as ordered in my signal timed 1109 C/19 is not to cross 37°N before 2200 C/20.

In a signal timed 2205 C/19, the above listed submarines were informed of the following by Capt. S.1; On reaching position in my 2059 C/19 proceed submerged at 3 knots on course 145° until 2130 hours. HMS Thrasher then return to patrol area keeping at least 10 miles to north east above course, and the four boats of the 10th S/M Flotilla proceed in accordance with my 1109 C/19. It is important that no submarine will be seen on the surface on the 20th.

In a signal timed 2259 C/19, the Capt. S.1 informed the submarines listed above of the following; My 2059 C/19. It is important that error in longitude should be reduced to a minimum. HMS Thrasher is to obtain accurate land fix at dawn, dive to position ordered to replace by 1200 hours or earlier if possible and then establish communication by SS/T to enable the other submarines to adjust their position. (19)

20 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 0401 C/20, the Capt. S.1, sent the following orders to HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 2059 C/19 shift patrol line 22 miles from 267°. My 2205 C/19 for 145° read 134°. HMS Thorn conform.

In a follow up signal timed 0455 C/20 sent by the Capt. S.1 to HMS Thorn, HMS Una and HMS P 31 and repeated to the other submarines the following orders were given; My 0401 C/20, HMS Thorn patrol along a line between positions 37°22'N, 20°15'E and 36°50'N, 20°59'E. HMS Una and HMS P 31 return to position 37°55'N, 19° 35'E and then steer 134°.

In a signal timed 1211 C/20, the following orders were sent by Capt. S.1; HMS Thorn, HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 return to Alexandria. Expected in accordance with previous orders. HMS Thrasher return to patrol area through 37°56'N, 20°03'E. (20)

21 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2135 C/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Area 33°00'N, 47°00'E, 23°14'E - 310° - 65 miles is not to be crossed during night 22nd/23rd. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) pass to southward of area. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passing to the south and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to the north of Gavdo Island. (20)

23 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1117 C/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Proceed to following positions from whence you are expected end of searched channel at the approx. time given but submarines are not to arrive off the boom after 2030 hours or before 0730 hours. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) through position 32°19'N, 29°08'E to arrive at (the end of the searched channel) 1401 C/27. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) through position 31°39'N, 29°02'E to arrive at 0459 C/28. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) through position 31°53'N, 28°55'E to arrive at 0801 C/28. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) through position 32°01'N, 28°59'E to arrive at 0501 C/29. (20)
28 Jun 1942 Around 0730 C/28, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Alexandria. (17)
29 Jun 1942 Around 2045 C/29, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Alexandria for Port Said. (21)
30 Jun 1942 Around 1755 C/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived at Port Said from Alexandria. (21)
3 Jul 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is docked in the floating dock at Port Said after the previous day an attempt to put her on the slipway had failed. (22)
5 Jul 1942 HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) is undocked. (22)
8 Jul 1942 Around 0555 C/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) departed Port Said for Haifa. (22)
10 Jul 1942 Around 0945 C/10, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) arrived at Haifa from Port Said. (22)

21 Jul 1942 Around 2000 C/21, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Haifa for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to proceed to Malta and to patrol North of Crete en-route.

28 Jul 1942 At 0515 C/28, when 12 miles north of Suda Bay, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 275° at a range of 5 (?, difficult to read in the patrol report) miles coming towards at speed. HMS Una crash dived to 80 feet.
At 0535 C/28, the HE of the destroyer faded out to the southward.
This was most likely the Italian destroyer Bersagliere which arrived at Suda Bay at 0710 B/28 (note the one hour time zone difference). Bersagliere had sailed from Navarino at 1945 B/27.

At 0645 C/28, a 'Generali-class' torpedo boat and a large armed yacht were seen patrolling three miles to the northward. No Asdic transmissions were heard. HMS Una continued her passage westwards towards the Anti-Kithera Channel.
This were probably the armed yacht Rovigno (451 GRT, built 1941). She was on minesweeping duty with the minesweeper RD 9 and they returned to Suda at 1235 B/28.

As for the 'Generali-class torpedo boat', there were no units of that class in this area. The torpedo boats Castore, Sagittario, Lince and Lupo were at Suda on that day but did not sortie. All did not even closely resemble a 'Generali-class torpedo boat'.

The destroyer Quintino Sella and the torpedo boat Lira had sailed from Leros at 1955 B/27-2000 B/27 and arrived at Suda at 0810 B-0817 B/28. It is possible that Una sighted one of them. Unless she mistook the minesweeper RD 9 for a torpedo boat. (23)
2 Aug 1942 Around 1215-1230 B/2, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta while HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar.
They were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1000 B/2. (17)

9 Aug 1942 Around 1100 B/9, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to carry out a special operation and to patrol to the east of Sicily.
She, and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), were swept out to the end of the swept channel by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (24)
9 Aug 1942 For the daily positions of HMS Una during her 11th war patrol see the map below.

11 Aug 1942 During the night of 11/12 August 1942, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) carried out special operation 'Why Not'. A group of six men were landed near Catania, Sicily, Italy to attack the local airfield. It was intended to land nine men from 'M section' of the Special Boat Squadron but one of the Folbots used in the landing was damaged by the heavy swell and three men returned to Una and the damaged Folbot was sunk.

HMS Una waited for the landing party to return but they failed to show up. They hadd found the airfield, which housed JU-88's but were detected by Italian patrols and shots were fired after which they returned to the beach and went out to sea. They however missed the rendezvous with the submarine. They remained at sea in their Folbots but also failed to show up at the second rendezvous. Their Folbots were destroyed by bad weather and they ended up in the water clinging to the wreckage. Eventually they were picked up by Italian fishermen which handed them over the Italian Army.

'M section' was made up of the following men; Captain G.E.A. Duncan, Black Watch, Lieutenant E. Newby, Black Watch, Lieutenant H.A.D. Buchanan, Grenadier Guards Sergeant W. Dunbar, Argyll & Southerland Highlanders Corporal J.H. Booth, Seaforth Higlanders Corporal H. Butler, Prince of Wales Volunteers Guardman W.P. Duffey, Coldstream Guards Bombardier J. Brittlebank, Royal Artillery Marine R.R. Didcot, Royal Marines
Six Folbots had been embarked for the operation, four to be used in the landing and two spare ones for re-embarkation if necessary. Also explosives had been embarked.

According to Italian sources, the three officers and two ratings were captured by Italian fishermen south of the mouth of the Simeto River. They had attempted to force the fishermen to take them to Malta. They did not oppose any resistance. A sixth men was captured by two Custom guards near the river. They had been armed with pistols and hand grenades. On the morning of the 13th, 60 mines and explosives were found abandoned in San Giuseppe Arena (Catania). Four explosives had caused slight damage tot he base of a pylon of electric low tension.
1934 B/11 - Una bottomed in 14 fathoms in position 150° - Catania Breakwater Light - 2.5 miles. Having made a periscope reconnaissance of the landing beach earlier.
2120 B/11 - Surfaced.
2130 B/11 - Placed the Folbots on the casing. It was just possible to open the fore hatch at full buoyancy without shipping serious quantities of water.
2140 B/11 - Proceeded slowly inshore trimmed down.

2208 B/11 - In estimated position 1500 yards - 090° from the landing position opposite San Giuseppe Church. Disembarked the Folbots. The first Folbot was caught by an unusually heavy sea which badly damaged the Folbot and it had to be sunk. The crew and the stores could be taken off. The remaining three Folbots were launches successfully. It was decided by Captain Duncan to leave Booth, Brittlebank and Didcot in Una.
2235 B/11 - The party was last seen making good headway towards the shore. Una withdrew to the seaward.
2300 B/11 - No sign of activity ashore. It was presumed the party had made a successful landing.
0015 B/12 - Dived and proceeded inshore.
0102 B/12 - Bottomed in 11 fathoms in position 090°, 1.8 miles from the mouth of the Simeto river.

0300 B/12 - Surfaced and adjusted position to keep between 1500 and 2500 yards off the river mouth which could just be seen. An Allied air raid was in progress over Catania and heavy AA fire was observed. All ground lights were extinguished.
0315 B/12 - The air raid was completed.

0415 B/12 - There had been no sign of the landing party. No explosions were seen or heard from the direction of the aerodrome. From 0350 B/12, Una had been flashing 'S' to seaward and with caution to landward, all to no avail.
0417 B/12 - HE was heard to the seaward.

0425 B/12 - More HE was heard to the northward. It was now 10 minutes after the last scheduled time of embarkation. It was also getting light and there seemed a possibility of a search being made to seaward.
0427 B/12 - Dived and proceeded to seaward.

It did not appear that an alarm had been raised ashore although the presence of surface craft pointed to the possibility that the alarm had been raided. It was consided that either the party had been taken prisoner or they had been delayed and abandoned the re-embarkation until a second rendezvous and had managed to partially achieve their object.
2053 B/12 - Una was now 20 miles offshore. A much needed battery charge was started.
0515 B/13 - Surfaced in position 37.25'N, 15.31'E and proceeded towards the southern end of Catania Bay.
1730 B/13 - Bottomed in 10 farthoms in position 050° - Torre del Agnone - 1.5 miles.
0030 B/14 - Surfaced and closed the landing beach.
0045 B/14 - In position 1500 yards from the pre-arranged point 090°, one mile from Torre del Agnone.
Three minutes before Capt. S.10's signal timed 1912 B/13 was received which after decoding contained the following orders; Cancel operation. Retire immediately. Report when safe to do so.
0120 B/14 - Withdrew to seaward. No signals or signs of activity had been seen on the shore.
0145 B/14 - Received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1318 B/13. [We have been unable to locate what this signal was about.]

0450 B/14 - Passed Una's signal timed 0445 B/14 to Capt. S.10 which stated the following; Your 1912 B/13. Three officers Three other ranks missing. No result observed. No sign at second rendezvous. Three did not land.
0500 B/14 - Dived and proceed to the patrol area off Cape Spartivento. (17)
15 Aug 1942 At 0600 B/15, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted what was thought to be the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) proceeding on course 125° at 12 knots.
At 0620 B/15, the hospital ship passed 2000 yards off track in position - 175° - Amandolea River - 10 miles.
This was indeed Gradisca which had sailed from Naples at 1130 B/14 and arrived at Tobruk at 0845 B/17. (17)

15 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1043 B/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) to leave patrol through positions 37°32'N, 16°13'E, 35°23'N, 15°14'E and 35°23'N, 14°12'E to rendezvous with sweeper at 1001 B/19 at end of the swept channel. (25)
17 Aug 1942 At 0910 B/15, to the east of Augusta, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) sighted two minesweepers approaching. Una turned to the south to avoid.
At 0915 B/15, these minesweepers were identified as A/S vessels. Both stopped and were listening. Range was only 1000 yards.
At 0919 B/15, they both turned towards and approached. Una went deep, prepared for a depth charge attack, and turned towards to pass underneath their sterns and thence to seaward.

At 0942 B/15, the HE of the enemy vessels faded. These A/S craft appeared to be diesel engined. About 200 tons, bridge aft, heavily camouflaged. No A/S transmissions were heard. They were both flying the Italian ensign. It is thought that they had been sent to the area due to Una being detected by shore hydrophones. (17)
19 Aug 1942 Around 1215 B/19, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 0930 B/19. (17)
30 Aug 1942 Around 1830 B/30, HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol West of Crete.
She was to proceed through position 35°37'N, 20°59'E en-route to her patrol area.
