10 Feb 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Cosala (4294 GRT, former Yugoslavian Serafin Topic, built 1913) near position 38°30'N, 16°40'E.
The damaged Italian ship is grounded, declared a total loss and sank during a storm.

0030 A/10 - Received an aircraft reported of one transport and a destroyer proceeding southwards from the Gulf of Taranto. Lt. Martin decided to try to intercept near Punta Stilo. Course was altered accordingly.
0547 A/10 - Dived in position 38°34'N, 16°49'E.

0600 A/10 - In the intended position of Cape Stilo. Nothing was however seen that morning of the expected convoy. Una remained close inshore until about 1500 A/10. During the forenoon a continuous air patrol was patrolling in the area.

The convoy reported by the aircraft was most probably the one made up of the transport Petrarca (Italian, 3329 GRT, built 1910) carrying ammunition escorted by the torpedo boat Pegaso. They had sailed from Taranto at 1801 A/9 for Tunis via Palermo. At 0700 A/10, Petrarca came under air attack and ran aground north of Crotone. She was finished off by HMS Una on 15 February 1943.
1455 A/10 - Sighted a northbound unladen 4000 tons merchant vessel escorted by one aircraft. Started attack.
1551 A/10 - Fired four torpedoes from 1000 to 1200 yards. One hit was obtained after just over a minute.
1556 A/10 - Sighted the target down by the stern. Una meanwhile withdrew to seaward to reload the torpedo tubes.
1958 A/10 - Surfaced in position 38°30'N, 16°56'E, and proceeded towards Cape Alice. (2)

12 Feb 1943 At 0550 A/12, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 39°15'N, 17°14'E and closed the land while dived. During the forenoon HMS Una attempted to gain a lee under Alice Point but a heavy sea was running right up the coast.

At 0910 A/12, in position 180° - Alica Point - 6 miles a small motor coaster of about 500 tons was sighted ashore beam on, heading south. This vessel was probably mistaken for a tanker on two occasions by the RAF. It is thought this ship must have suffered a severe bearing in the weather conditions of the past few days.
This was the Italian Emilio (191 GRT, 1912) which had ran aground at 1700/11 February near Torre Melissa, south of Alice Point and was a total loss. (2)

13 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1707 A/13, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) to carefully investigate the position 10 miles south of Alice Point where aircraft reported a tanker stopped at 1320 A today. The enemy vessel is probably beached and has vessels standing by protecting her. (48)

13 Feb 1943 At 0648 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 39°23'N, 17°12'E and commenced to close the coast. It was intended to investigate the wreck seen the previous day with a view of torpedoing her if salvage seemed possible.
At 0730 A/13, enemy aircraft commenced an intensive search of the area.
At 0835 A/13, a Generali-class torpedo boat was sighted which joined the aircraft in it's A/S search.

This was the Italian torpedo boat Angelo Bassini which had sailed from Crotone at 0620 A/13 to assist the transport Petrarca which had run aground near Strongoli (Punta Alice area). She reached the area at 0751 A/13 and conducted an A/S search and returned to Crotone at 0830 A/14.

At 1300 A/13, an A/S schooner joined in the search which continued until darkness. At one time the torpedo boat passed very near, almost directly overhead. The vessels were not transmitting on Asdic but the impression was that the presence of the submarine was known. The sea was glassy calm during the forenoon and as HMS Una withdrew to the north-eastwards, the enemy A/S vessels moved with her.
At 1615 A/13, an Allied air raid on Crotone was observed.
At 1645 A/13, a small schooner, probably A/S fitted and in ballast, slowly passed in the direction of Alice Point.
At 1847 A/13, HMS Una surfaced and proceeded towards Trionto Point.

At 2006 A/13, HMS Una received a signal timed 1707 A/13, in which the Capt. S.10 ordered her to carefully investigate the position 10 miles south of Alice Point where aircraft reported a tanker stopped at 1320 A today. The enemy vessel is probably beached and has vessels standing by protecting her.

The schooners sighted on this day may have been part of the group operating from Crotone during the day: Carmelo Solonia (?), V 265 / Cesena (105 GRT, built 1921), Zueca P. (?) and / or Francesco di Gregorio (?). (2)

14 Feb 1943 At 0540 A/14, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) dived in position 39°18'N, 17°18'E and proceeded submerged to investigate the wreck seen on the 12th. The wind had increased to force 7 during the early hours of the morning, making depth keeping very difficult.

At 0840 A/14, the vessel was sighted and she was closed to investigate. She proved to be a standard cargo vessel of about 4000 to 5000 tons painted light and dark grey in stripes. The vessel appeared completely intact though a little down by the stern. She was heading south and was laden. Considered that salvage was comparatively easy and that the vessel should be torpedoed. The prevailing southerly gale made it impossible to do so on this day.
At 0920 A/14, HMS Una withdrew to seaward surfacing at 1903 A/14. (2)

15 Feb 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) torpedoed and sank the (already damaged and grounded) Italian transport Petrarca (3329 GRT, built 1910) about 10 nautical miles north of Crotone, Calabria, Italy in position 39°16'N, 17°08'E.

0545 A/15 - Dived in position 39°03'N, 17°19'E and started to close the beached vessel. The area was patrolled by an aircraft, an A/S schooner, an A/S brigantine and an armed yacht. Salvage operations were seen to be in progress. As the sea was flat calm Una was very visible from the air a periscope depth. It was decided to wait to attack after sunset.
1700 A/15 - Closed the target while dived.

1704 A/15 - Fired three torpedoes from about 3000 yards. The torpedoes were seen to run straight to the target but the result could not be seen as depth control was lost soon afterwards and HMS Una hit the bottom. A hit was heard given a running range of 2700 yards.
1820 A/15 - A loud explosion was heard from the direction of the target and it was hoped this was the vessel blowing up. Whilst withdrawing HE was heard to seaward but no counter attack developed.
2359 A/15 - Surfaced and ran to the eastward for an hour before heading for Malta. (Una was to proceed through positions 38°38'N, 17°52'E, 35°20'N, 15°17'E and 35°20'N, 14°18'E on the way back.) (2)

16 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 0505 A/16, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 of the following; 24 hours late. 10th Feb. sank 4000 ton north bound merchant ship near Cape Stilo. P.M./15th torpedoed similar vessel beached south of Alice. One torpedo remains. A weather report was also included in the signal. (49)
19 Feb 1943 Around 1045 A/19, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (2)
1 Mar 1943 Around 1400 A/1, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Malta for the U.K. where she was to refit.
She was to pass through position 38°01'N, 11°30'E (west of Marettimo) at 0500 A/3. Further onward routing would be signalled by Capt. S.8.
On board were only two MK II torpedoes.

In a signal timed 1652 A/1, the Capt. S.8 sent the following instructions; Proceed through the following positions; (A) 38°17'N, 11°10'E at 1300 A/3, (B) 38°17'N, 01°37'E at 1000 A/7, (C) 135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles at 1000 A/8, (D) 180° - Cape de Gata - 5 miles at 0500 A/9th and (E) 180° - Cape Calaburras - 5 miles at 0600 A/10 to arrive at Gibraltar 1100 A/10. Una was not to pass position 'E' before 0600 A/10th owing to Allied A/S patrols. Una was to shift normal W/T watch to Algiers broadcast at 0500 A/3 and to Gibraltar broadcast at 0500 A/8th. Submerged bombing restrictions arranged 10 miles either side of your route from 35 miles ahead to 35 miles astern of your estimated position. Total bombing restrictions arranged on the 10th.

3 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1140 A/3 the Capt. S.8 informed HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that four destroyers would pass through position 330° - Cape Serrat - 11 miles at 1930 A/3 and proceed through positions 38°08'N, 10°50'E and 38°03'N, 11°22'E thence two destroyers would proceed to Malta and two would return to Bone. This referred to the destroyers HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Loyal (Lt.Cdr. H.E.F. Tweedie, DSC, RN) (which were to return to Bone) and the escort destroyer HMS Easton (Lt. C.W. Malins, RN) and Miaoulis (?) (which were to proceed to Malta).

In a signal timed 1437 A/3, the Vice-Admiral Gibraltar informed HMS Una that the Spanish merchant vessels Cristina (2135 GRT, built 1921) and Monte Monjuich (4889 GRT, built 1922) would leave Genoa on the 5th and 6th March respectively for Gibraltar and were not to be molested. (50)

6 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1411 A/6, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was informed by the Capt. S.8 that HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) would departed Gibraltar for Algiers at 1600 A/7 routed through positions 180° - Cape Calaburras - 15 miles at 2125 A/7, 36°37'N, 04°00'W at 0230 A/8, 180° - Cape de Gata - 15 miles at 0013 A/9 and 135° - Cape Palos - 15 miles at 2143 A/9.

In a signal timed 1544 A/6, the Flag Officer Gibrsaltar, informed HMS Una that two Spanish submarines are exercising in vicinity of Cartagena March 9th to 11th as follows: General Mola, trials by day off Cape Tinos. C 4 in Masarron area torpedo firing exercises. (51)

7 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1817 A/7, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) were informed that HMS Laforey (Capt. R.M.J. Hutton, DSO, RN) and HMS Lookout (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) left Algiers at 1044 A/7th to proceed to Gibraltar at 22 knots. HMS Tartar (Cdr. St J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN) and ORP Blyskawica (Kmdr.ppor. (Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski) would leave Philippeville at 2000 A/7 to proceed to Gibraltar at approximately 28 knots. All are informed of your movements. (51)
9 Mar 1943 At 0322 A/9, north-west of Algiers, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) and took avoiding action.
Apparently HMS Unshaken did not see HMS Una. (2)
10 Mar 1943 Around 1100 A/10, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Malta. (2)
12 Mar 1943 HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) conducted D/G trials at Gibraltar. (2)
13 Mar 1943 Around 1245 A/13, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Gibraltar for Holy Loch.
On the 11th, two additional torpedoes (Mk. VIII's) had been embarked in addition to the two MK. II torpedoes that had been embarked at Malta.

24 Mar 1943 Around 1400 A/24, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) joined HMS P 556 (Lt.Cdr. F.H. Sherwood, RCNVR) and USS Blackfish (T/Cdr. J.F. Davidson, USN) which came from Falmouth. They were escorted by HMS Lundy (T/Lt. S.J. Lavis, RNR).
Around 1820 A/24, HMS Lundy parted company and shortly afterwards HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) joined. (52)
26 Mar 1943 Around 1300 A/26, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Gibraltar. (2)

28 Mar 1943 Around 1715 A/28, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Holy Loch for Blyth. She was escorted by HMS Alecto (Lt.Cdr. H.A.L. Marsham, RN) until 0915 A/30 when HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) took over the escort. (2)
31 Mar 1943 Around 1620 A/31, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) arrived at Blyth from Holy Loch. At Blyth she is to be taken in hand for refit. (2)
4 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) is wiped (degaussed) at Blyth. (53)
5 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) ended her refit at Blyth. (53)
7 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted a basin dive trial at Blyth followed by D/G trials. (53)
9 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted diving and gunnery trials off Blyth. (53)
10 Aug 1943 HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) conducted diving trials off Blyth. (53)

16 Mar 1944 HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) is recalled from Campbeltown to Rothesay to take on board fuel and provisions as she is sent on patrol off Norway as the British think that the German battleship Tirpitz is going to leave Northern Norway for repairs in Germany.

HMS Una departed for Lerwick at 0830 A/16 together with HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) and HrMs Zeehond (Lt.Cdr. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN). At 1300 A/16, off Sanda Island, they were joined by their escort, the corvette HMCS Camrose (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.R. Pavillard, RCNR) which remained with them until around 1545 A/17. HMS Safari and HMS Una continued onwards in company towards Lerwick while HrMs Zeehond proceeded independently towards Lerwick. (2)
18 Mar 1944 Around 0850 A/18, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) arrived at Lerwick for fuel.
At 1135 A/18, she departed for 20th her war patrol. Patrol area was the south-west coast of Norway off Egersund passing through position 60°09'N, 03°25'E en-route.
HMS Una 20th war patrol click here for bigger map (2)

21 Mar 1944 As it has become clear that the German battleship Tirpitz is not on the move back to Germany, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) is recalled from patrol in the Flag Officer Submarines signal timed 2308 A/20 which was received at 0022 A/21. (2)
23 Mar 1944 Around 1605 A/23, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) ended her 20th war patrol at Lerwick. (2)

24 Mar 1944 Around 0210 A/24, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) departed Lerwick for Rothesay. She made the passage together with HMS Truant (Lt. E.C. Croswell, DSC, RN), HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN), HMS Vivid (Lt. J.C. Varley, RN) and HrMs Zeehond (Lt.Cdr. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN). They were escorted by the British minesweeper HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) C.M. Norman, RN). (2)
27 Mar 1944 Around 0120 A/27, HMS Una (Lt. C.A.J. Nicholl, RN) arrived at Rothesay from Lerwick. (2)
